options.en: It allowed people over 21 years old to vote
It guaranteed that no one could be held prisoner unlawfully
It abolished slavery throughout the British Empire
It abolished a number of taxes on imported goods in the UK

explanation_en: The Habeas Corpus Act became law in 1679. This was a very important piece of legislation which remains relevant today. The Act guaranteed that no one could be held prisoner unlawfully. Every prisoner has a right to a court hearing.

question_en: What British artist was awarded the Turner Prize?
explanation_en: Damien Hirst .

question_en: How many members has the Council of Europe?
explanation_en: The Council of Europe is separated from the EU. It has 47 member countries , including the UK, and is responsible for the protection and promotion of human rights in those countries.

question_en: In the UK, Members of the Parliament (MPs) are elected on the basis of:

options.en: First past the post system (the candidate who gets the most votes)

explanation_en: MPs are elected through a system called ‘first past the post’ . In  constituency, the candidate who gets the most votes is elected.

question_en: Which of the following statements regarding television in Northern Ireland is TRUE?

options.en: Some programmes are broadcasted in Irish Gaelic
All programmes are broadcasted in English
There are no specific programmes to Northern Ireland

explanation_en: There are programmes specific to Northern Ireland and some programmes broadcast in Irish Gaelic.

question_en: In the second half of the 19th century there was an important group of artists who painted detailed pictures on religious or literary themes in bright colours. These were known as:
explanation_en: The Pre-Raphaelites were an important group of artists in the second half of the 19th century. They painted detailed pictures on religious or literary themes in bright colours. The group included Holman Hunt, Dante Gabriel Rossetti and Sir John Millais.

question_en: The Muslim festival known as Eid ul Adha reminds Muslims of their own commitment to God:
explanation_en: Eid ul Adha reminds Muslims of their own commitment to God.

question_en: What was the population of the UK in 1901?
explanation_en: The population of the UK in 1901 was 40 million people .

question_en: People in the UK do NOT have to pay tax on:
explanation_en: People in the UK have to pay tax on their income, which includes: wages from paid employment, profits from self-employment, taxable benefits, pensions and income from property, savings and dividends.

question_en: Which British scientist co-invented the MRI (magnetic resonance imaging) scanner?
explanation_en: Sir Peter Mansfield (1933-), a British scientist, is the co-inventor of the MRI (magnetic resonance imaging) scanner. This enables doctors and researchers to obtain exact and non-invasive images of human internal organs and has revolutionised diagnostic medicine.

question_en: Which political party called a referendum on the UK’s membership of the European Union?
explanation_en: The Conservative government called a referendum on the UK’s membership of the European Union.

question_en: Which of the following is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and a popular area for walkers?
explanation_en: Parts of Hadrian’s Wall , including the forts of Housesteads and Vindolanda is a popular area for walkers and is a UNESCO (United Nations Educational Scientific and Cultural Organization) World Heritage Site.

question_en: When did hereditary peers lose the automatic right to attend the House of Lords?
explanation_en: Since 1999 , hereditary peers have lost the automatic right to attend the House of Lords. They now elect a few of their number to represent them in the House of Lords.

question_en: Which charity works for the preservation of buildings in England?
explanation_en: In the UK, many parts of the countryside and places of interest are kept open by the National Trust in England, Wales and Northern Ireland and the National Trust for Scotland. Both are charities that work to preserve important buildings, coastline and countryside in the UK.

question_en: What name is given to the new social classes that appeared after the Black Death plague and who owned large areas of land?
explanation_en: Following the Black Death, the smaller population meant there was less need to grow cereal crops. There were labour shortages and peasants began to demand higher wages. New social classes appeared, including owners of large areas of land (later called the gentry ), and people left the countryside to live in the towns. In the towns, growing wealth led to the development of a strong middle class.

question_en: Which of the following is a traditional Welsh food?
explanation_en: Welsh cakes are a traditional Welsh snack made from flour, dried fruits and spices, and served either hot or cold.

question_en: Which of the following was one of the most famous battles of the Hundred Years War?
explanation_en: One of the most famous battles of the Hundred Years War was the Battle of Agincourt in 1415, where King Henry V’s vastly outnumbered English army defeated the French. The English left France in the 1450s.

question_en: During the Middle Ages, England was an important trading nation and people came to England from abroad to trade and also to work. Where did the engineers come from?
explanation_en: During the Middle Ages, people came to England from abroad to trade and also to work. Many had special skills, such as weavers from France, engineers from Germany , glass manufacturers from Italy and canal builders from Holland.

question_en: Who was the captain of the English football team that won the World Cup in 1966?
explanation_en: Bobby Moore (1941-93) captained the English football team that won the World Cup in 1966.

question_en: Membership to the Commonwealth  is compulsory for its members:
explanation_en: Membership to the Commonwealth is voluntary.

question_en: How many verdicts are possible in trials in Scotland?
explanation_en: The jury has to listen to the evidence presented at the trial and then decide a verdict of ‘guilty’ or ‘not guilty’ based of what they have heard. In Scotland, a third verdict of ‘not proven’ is also possible.

question_en: How many wives did Henry VIII have?
explanation_en: Henry VIII had six wives .

question_en: Which of the following charities works with sick animals?
explanation_en: The PDSA is the People’s Dispensary for Sick Animals.

question_en: Which of the following is the responsibility of the Home Secretary?
explanation_en: The Home Secretary is responsible for crime, policing and immigration .

question_en: Which of the following sports can be traced as far back as the Roman times?
explanation_en: There is a very long history of horse racing in Britain, with evidence of events taking place as far back as Roman times.

question_en: When did the English become the preferred language of the royal court and Parliament?
explanation_en: By 1400 , in England, official documents were being written in English, and English had become the preferred language of the royal court and Parliament.

question_en: When is St Andrews Day, patron saint of Scotland?
explanation_en: St Andrews Day, patron saint of Scotland, is celebrated on the 30th of November .

question_en: Which organisation has been organising ‘The Proms’ since 1927?
explanation_en: The Proms is an eight-week summer season of orchestral classical music that takes place in various venues, including the Royal Albert Hall in London. It has been in various venues, including the Royal Albert Hall in London. It has been organised by the British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC) since 1927.

question_en: Which of the following organisations has no power to make laws but draws up conventions and charters, including the European Convention on Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms?
explanation_en: The Council of Europe is responsible for the protection and promotion of human rights in those countries. It has no power to make laws but draws up conventions and charters, the most well-known of which is the European Convention on Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms, usually called the European Convention on Human Rights.

question_en: Where is Snowdonia National Park located?
explanation_en: Snowdonia is a national park in North Wales .

question_en: What actions did Henry VII take after his victory in the Wars of the Roses?

options.en: Reduced the power of the nobles
Broke away with the Church of Rome
Increased the power of the nobles

explanation_en: After his victory in the Wars of the Roses, Henry VII wanted to make sure that England remained peaceful and that his position as king was secure. He deliberately strengthened the central administration of England and reduced the power of the nobles.

question_en: Which Court deals with the most serious civil cases in Scotland?
explanation_en: More serious civil cases – for example, when a large amount of compensation is being claimed – are dealt with in the High Court of England, Wales and Northern Ireland. In Scotland, they are dealt with in the Court of Session in Edinburgh.

question_en: The leader of the opposition leads his or her party in pointing out what they see as the government’s (choose TWO options):
explanation_en: The leader of the opposition leads his or her party in pointing out what they see as the government’s failures and weaknesses .

question_en: Who became Prime Minister of the UK in May 2010?
explanation_en: In May 2010, and for the first time in the UK since February 1974, no political party won an overall majority in the General Election. The Conservative and Liberal Democrat parties formed a coalition and the leader of the Conservative Party, David Cameron , became Prime Minister.

question_en: The Reform Act of 1832 abolished the old pocket and rotten boroughs.
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: What is the minimum age requirement to drive a moped in the UK?
explanation_en: You need to be at least 16 years old to ride a moped, and there are other age requirements and special tests for driving large vehicles.

question_en: Which of the following statements is true?

options.en: Great Britain refers only to England, Wales, Northern Ireland, not to Scotland.
Great Britain refers only to England, Northern Ireland and Scotland, not to Wales.
Great Britain refers to England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland.
Great Britain refers only to England, Scotland and Wales, not to Northern Ireland.

explanation_en: Great Britain refers only to England, Scotland and Wales, not to Northern Ireland.

question_en: How many countries formed the European Economic Community (EEC) when it was first created in 1957?
explanation_en: The European Economic Community (EEC), was set up by six western European countries (Belgium, France, Germany, Italy, Luxembourg and the Netherlands) who signed the Treaty of Rome on 25 March 1957.

question_en: Northern Ireland and Wales have their own banknotes, which are valid everywhere in the UK. However, shop and businesses do not have to accept them:
explanation_en: Northern Ireland and Scotland have their own banknotes, which are valid everywhere in the UK. However, shops and businesses do not have to accept them.

question_en: What is donated blood used for?

options.en: To help people with a wide range of injuries and illnesses
Blood cannot be donated in the UK
To carry out research with your DNA
To be stored in the hospital in case you need it in the future

explanation_en: Donated blood is used by hospitals to help people with a wide range of injuries and illnesses .

question_en: How often are the general elections held in the UK?
explanation_en: MPs are elected at a General Election, which is held at least every five years .

question_en: Where is the Grand National horse racing event celebrated?
explanation_en: The Grand National horse racing event is celebrated in Aintree near Liverpool.

question_en: What was Charles Chaplin famous for?
explanation_en: Sir Charles (Charlie) Chaplin became famous in silent movies for his tramp character and was one of many British actors to make a career in Hollywood.

question_en: What was Boudicca, the queen of the Iceni in what is now eastern England, known for?
explanation_en: One of the tribal leaders who fought against the Romans was Boudicca, the queen of the Iceni in what is now eastern England.

question_en: What percentage of the British population has a parent or grandparent born outside of the UK as a result of post-war immigration?
explanation_en: Post-war immigration means that nearly 10% of the population has a parent or grandparent born outside the UK.

question_en: When was the hovercraft invented?
explanation_en: Sir Christopher Cockerell (1910-99), a British inventor, invented the hovercraft in the 1950s .

question_en: Who is the author of the famous play ‘Macbeth’?
explanation_en: Macbeth is a famous play by William Shakespeare .

question_en: What animal was cloned by scientists Sir Ian Wilmot and Keith Campbell?
explanation_en: In 1996, two British scientists, Sir Ian Wilmot (1944-) and Keith Campbell (1954-2012), led a team which was the first to succeed in cloning a mammal, Dolly the sheep .

question_en: What is the name of the building where the Northern Ireland Assembly meet?
explanation_en: The Northern Ireland Assembly building is known as Stormont .

question_en: Which of the following is NOT a function of the House of Lords?

options.en: Check laws that have been passed by the House of Commons

explanation_en: The House of Lords can suggest amendments or propose new laws, which are then discussed by MPs. The House of Lords checks laws that have been passed by the House of Commons to ensure they are fit for purpose. It also holds the government to account to make sure that it is working in the best interests of the people.

question_en: Which of the following is NOT a World Heritage Site?

options.en: The forts of Housesteads and Vindolanda

explanation_en: The London Eye is not a World Heritage Site.

question_en: Which famous murder-mystery play has been running in the west end since 1952 and has had the longest initial run of any show in history?
explanation_en: The Mousetrap , a murder-mystery play by Dame Agatha Christie, has been running in the west end since 1952 and has had the longest initial run of any show in history.

question_en: When was the Giant’s Causeway formed?
explanation_en: The Giant’s Causeway was formed about 50 million years ago .

question_en: Who was the first person to sail single-handed around the world without stopping?
explanation_en: A British sailor, Sir Francis Chichester, was the first person to sail singlehanded around the world, in 1966/67. Two years later, Sir Robin Knox- Johnston became the first person to do this without stopping.

question_en: What British writer created the fictional detective Sherlock Holmes?
explanation_en: Sir Arthur Conan Doyle was a Scottish doctor and writer. He was best known for his stories about Sherlock Holmes, who was one of the first fictional detectives.

question_en: How can you visit the  Senedd?
explanation_en: To book guided tours or seats in the public galleries for the Senedd you can contact the Senedd Booking Service on 0300 200 6565, email contact@senedd.wales or visit www.senedd.wales for an online booking form.

question_en: How were Elizabeth I and ‘Mary, Queen of Scots’ related?
explanation_en: ‘Mary, Queen of Scots’ was Elizabeth I’s cousin.

question_en: Choose the correct answer. The Middle Ages were:

options.en: A period of peace in Britain
A period where liberal ideas were introduced in Britain
A period where new ideas about politics and philosophy were introduced in Britain

explanation_en: The Middle Ages (or the medieval period) was a time of almost constant war .

question_en: What does the NATO stand for?
explanation_en: NATO stands for The North Atlantic Treaty Organisation .

question_en: What characterised the people from the Bronze Age?

options.en: They were farmers who lived in large villages

explanation_en: The people of the Bronze Age were accomplished metalworkers who made many beautiful objects in bronze and gold, including tools, ornaments and weapons.

question_en: Where is the National Horseracing Museum located?
explanation_en: The National Horseracing Museum is located in Newmarket, Suffolk .

question_en: In which of the following matters the Scottish Parliament CANNOT legislate?
explanation_en: The matters on which the Scottish Parliament can legislate include: civil and criminal law, health, education, planning and additional tax-raising powers.

question_en: Which of the following is a popular showcase of mainly theatre and comedy performances and the biggest and most well-known festival that takes place during the Edinburgh Festival?
explanation_en: The Fringe is a showcase of mainly theatre and comedy performances. It often shows experimental work.The biggest and most well-known being the Edinburgh Festival Fringe.

question_en: Does Britain have a constitutional monarchy?
explanation_en: The UK has a constitutional monarchy.

question_en: Which Scottish city is known as the home of golf?
explanation_en: St. Andrews in Scotland is known as the home of golf.

question_en: Which court deals with the most serious cases of children aged 10 to 17 in England, Wales and Northern Ireland?
explanation_en: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland, if an accused person is aged 10 to 17, the case is normally heard in a Youth Court in front of up to three specially trained magistrates or a District Judge. The most serious cases will go the Crown Court .

question_en: What is the name of the best preserved prehistoric village in northern Europe and which origin traces back to the Stone Age?
explanation_en: Skara Brae on Orkney, off the north coast of Scotland, is the best preserved prehistoric village in northern Europe, and has helped archaeologists to understand more about how people lived near the end of the Stone Age.

question_en: When was the National Trust founded?
explanation_en: The National Trust was founded in 1895 by three volunteers.

question_en: After the Black Death, new social classes appeared in England, including owners of large areas of land known as:
explanation_en: Following the Black Death, the smaller population meant there was less need to grow cereal crops. There were labour shortages and peasants began to demand higher wages. New social classes appeared, including owners of large areas of land (later called the gentry ), and people left the countryside to live in the towns. In the towns, growing wealth led to the development of a strong middle class.

question_en: Elizabeth I was the younger daughter of which King?
explanation_en: Elizabeth I was the younger daughter of Henry VIII .

question_en: Which of the following operas was written by Gilbert and Sullivan?
explanation_en: In the 19th century, Gilbert and Sullivan wrote comic operas, often making fun of popular culture and politics. These operas include HMS Pinafore, The Pirates of Penzance and The Mikado .

question_en: Where is Loch Lomond and the Trossachs National Park located?
explanation_en: Loch Lomond and the Trossachs National Park is located in the west of Scotland .

question_en: Who is married to the King of the United Kingdom?
explanation_en: The King of the United Kingdom is married to Camila, the Queen Consort.

question_en: By what TWO other names is the Church of England known?
explanation_en: The official Church of the state is the Church of England (called the Anglican Church in other countries and the Episcopal Church in Scotland and the United States).

question_en: Who was the first British Prime Minister?
explanation_en: The first man to British Prime Minister was Sir Robert Walpole , who was Prime Minister from 1721 to 1742.

question_en: What day does Lent start?
explanation_en: Lent begins on Ash Wednesday.

question_en: How old do you need to be to apply for a free TV licence?
explanation_en: People over 75 can apply for a free TV licence and blind people can get a 50% discount.

question_en: What time do pubs usually open on Sundays in the UK?
explanation_en: Pubs are usually open during the day from 11.00 am (12 noon on Sundays).

question_en: What is the official home of the Prime Minister?
explanation_en: The official home of the Prime Minister is 10 Downing Street, in central London, near the Houses of Parliament. He or she also has a country house outside London called Chequers.

question_en: When did the Conservative government called a referendum on the UK’s membership of the European Union?
explanation_en: The Conservative government called a referendum on the UK’s membership of the European Union on the 23rd June 2016.

question_en: Which British sportswoman won two gold medals for running in the 2004 Olympic Games?
explanation_en: Dame Kelly Holmes won two gold medals for running in the 2004 Olympic Games. She has held a number of British and European records.

question_en: What percentage of the total British population is located in England alone?
explanation_en: The population is very unequally distributed over the four parts of the UK. England more or less consistently makes up 84% of the total population, Wales around 5%, Scotland just over 8%, and Northern Ireland less than 3%.

question_en: What was the population of the UK in 2010?
explanation_en: The population of the UK in 2010 was just over 62 million people.

question_en: Which court deals with cases involving personal injury, family matters, breaches of contract and divorce in England and Wales?
explanation_en: County Courts deal with a wide range of civil disputes. These include people trying to get back money that is owed to them, cases involving personal injury, family matters, breaches of contract, and divorce. In Scotland, most of these matters are dealt with in the Sheriff Court.

question_en: What is the name of the building where the Scottish Parliament meet?
explanation_en: In Scotland the elected members, called MSPs, meet in the Scottish Parliament building at Holyrood in Edinburgh.

question_en: Which flower is associated with England?
explanation_en: The rose is the flower associated with England.

question_en: Who was Robert Burns?
explanation_en: Known in Scotland as ‘The Bard’, Robert Burns was a Scottish poet .

question_en: In which period did British Film studios flourish?
explanation_en: British film studios flourished in the 1930s.

question_en: Who defeated the French at the battle of Agincourt in 1415?
explanation_en: One of the most famous battles of the Hundred Years War was the Battle of Agincourt in 1415, where King Henry V ’s vastly outnumbered English army defeated the French.

question_en: Which is the most famous tennis tournament hosted in Britain and which takes place each year at the All England Lawn Tennis and Croquet Club?
explanation_en: The most famous tournament hosted in Britain is The Wimbledon Championships , which takes place each year at the All England Lawn Tennis and Croquet Club.

question_en: What is the minimum age required for a citizen to stand for public office?
explanation_en: Most citizens of the UK, the Irish republic or the Commonwealth aged 18 or over can stand for public office.

question_en: Who makes sure that the opposition has a guaranteed amount of time to debate issues which it chooses during political debates?
explanation_en: The Speaker keeps order during political debates to make sure the rules are followed. This includes making sure the opposition has a guaranteed amount of time to debate issues which it chooses.

question_en: Citizens of Liechtenstein can drive in the UK for as long as their driving license is valid:
explanation_en: If your driving licence if from a country in the European Union (EU), Iceland, Liechtenstein or Norway, you can drive in the UK for as long as your licence is valid.

question_en: Which British Prime Minister nationalized major industries including coal and steel?
explanation_en: Clement Attlee ’s government undertook the nationalisation of major industries (like coal and steel), created the National Health Service and implemented many of Beveridge’s plans for a stronger welfare state. Attlee also introduced measures to improve the conditions of workers.

question_en: Who was the first Archbishop of Canterbury?
explanation_en: St Augustine became the first Archbishop of Canterbury.

question_en: What is the money raised from the Income Tax used for (choose THREE options)?
explanation_en: Money raised from income tax pays for government services such as roads, education, police and the armed forces .

question_en: What name is given to the clock tower at the Houses of Parliament in honour of Queen Elizabeth II’s Diamond Jubilee in 2012?
explanation_en: The clock tower at the Houses of Parliament in London is named ‘Elizabeth Tower’ in honour of Queen Elizabeth II’s Diamond Jubilee in 2012.

question_en: When were the Forced Marriage Protection Orders introduced for England, Wales and Northern Ireland, which aimed at protecting a person from being forced into a marriage, or to protect a person in a forced marriage?
explanation_en: Forced Marriage Protection Orders were introduced in 2008 for England, Wales and Northern Ireland under the Forced Marriage (Civil Protection) Act 2007.

question_en: Whose details are NOT listed in the Phone Book?
explanation_en: MPs, Senedd members (SMs) and members of the Scottish Parliament (MSPs) are also listed in The Phone Book, published by BT. The King is not.

question_en: Which of the following words comes from the Norman French?
explanation_en: Some words in modern English – for example, ‘park’ and ‘beauty’ – are based on Norman French words.

question_en: How are the official reports where the Parliament’s proceedings are published called?
explanation_en: Proceedings in Parliament are broadcast on television and published in official reports called Hansard .

question_en: How many Senedd members does the Welsh government have?
explanation_en: The Welsh government has 60 Senedd members (SMs) and elections are held every four years using a form of proportional representation.

question_en: Catherine Parr, the sixth wife of Henry VIII, was a cousin of Anne Boleyn:
explanation_en: Catherine Howard was a cousin of Anne Boleyn.

question_en: Who deals with cases involving personal injury, family matters, breaches of contract and divorce in Scotland?
explanation_en: County Courts deal with a wide range of civil disputes. These include people trying to get back money that is owed to them, cases involving personal injury, family matters, breaches of contract, and divorce. In Scotland, most of these matters are dealt with in the Sheriff Court .

question_en: What was the symbol of York during the Wars of the Roses?
explanation_en: The symbol of York during the Wars of the Roses was a white rose .

question_en: According to the 2011 Census, what percentage of the population identified themselves as Jewish?
explanation_en: According to the 2011 Census, less than 0.5% of people identify themselves as Jewish.

question_en: During which movement the Parliament passed laws giving women the right to equal pay and made it illegal for employees to discriminate against women because of their gender:
explanation_en: The Swinging Sixties was a time when the Parliament passed new laws giving women the right to equal pay and made it illegal for employers to discriminate against women because of their gender.

question_en: What name is given to the members of the House of Lords?
explanation_en: Members of the House of Lords, known as peers , are not elected by the people and do not represent a constituency.

question_en: Where can you find a statue of Boudicca, the queen of the Iceni and one of the tribal leaders who fought against the Romans?
explanation_en: There is a statue of Boudicca, the queen of the Iceni, on Westminster Bridge in London, near the Houses of Parliament.

question_en: Which of the following castles is located in Scotland?
explanation_en: Crathes Castle and Inveraray Castle are located in Scotland.

question_en: For how long was Britain a republic under the leadership of Oliver Cromwell?
explanation_en: Britain was a republic under the leadership of Oliver Cromwell for 11 years .

question_en: Magistrates and Justices of the Peace (JPs) are members of:
explanation_en: Magistrates and Justices of the Peace (JPs) are members of the local community .

question_en: Which British explorer mapped the coast of Australia?
explanation_en: Captain James Cook mapped the coast of Australia and a few colonies were established there.

question_en: How many members does a jury have in Scotland?
explanation_en: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland a jury has 12 members, and in Scotland a jury has 15 members .

question_en: Who can get a 50% discount on their TV licences?
explanation_en: People over 75 can apply for a free TV licence and blind people can get a 50% discount.

question_en: Which British city is famous for its celebration of Diwali?
explanation_en: There is a famous celebration of Diwali in Leicester .

question_en: Which of the following British actresses has NOT won an Oscar?
explanation_en: Recent British actors to have won Oscars include Colin Firth, Sir Antony Hopkins, Dame Judi Dench, Kate Winslet and Tilda Swinton. Emily Watson has not won an oscar .

question_en: Which of the following sports has a long association with royalty?
explanation_en: Horse racing has a long association with royalty.

question_en: Hanukkah is a Jewish festival celebrated to remember the Jews’ struggle for religious freedom.
explanation_en: Hanukkah is in November or December and is celebrated for eight days. It is to remember the Jews’ struggle for religious freedom.

question_en: How can you visit the Northern Ireland Assembly (choose TWO options)?

options.en: Arranging a tour through the visitor services

explanation_en: There are two ways to arrange a visit to the Northern Ireland Assembly at Stormont. You can either contact the Education Service (details are on the Northern Ireland Assembly website at http://www.niassembly.gov.uk) or contact an MLA .

question_en: Most citizens of the UK, the Irish republic or the Commonwealth aged 18 or over can stand for public office, except for (choose THREE options):

options.en: People found guilty of certain criminal offences

explanation_en: Members of the Commonwealth aged 18 or over can stand for public office. There are some exceptions, including: members of the armed forces, civil servants and people found guilty of certain criminal offences .

question_en: What were the main occupations of the people during the Iron Age?
explanation_en: During the Iron Age most people were farmers, craft workers or warriors .

question_en: To look after yourself and your family is one of the responsibilities that you will have as a permanent resident or citizen of the UK.
explanation_en: This statement is TRUE .

question_en: When is the electoral register updated?
Which of the following is the official church of the state?

options.en: The Church of the United Kingdom

explanation_en: The official Church of the state is the Church of England (called the Anglican Church in other countries and the Episcopal Church in Scotland and the United States).

question_en: Where does the Scottish Parliament sit?
explanation_en: In Scotland the elected members, called MSPs, meet in the Scottish Parliament building at Holyrood in Edinburgh .

question_en: Who became one of the most popular monarchs in English history, particularly after 1588, when the English defeated the Spanish Armada?
explanation_en: Elizabeth I became one of the most popular monarchs in English history, particularly after 1588, when the English defeated the Spanish Armada (a large fleet of ships), which had been sent by Spain to conquer England and restore Catholicism.

question_en: What was the population of the UK in 1700?
explanation_en: The population of the UK in 1700 was 5 million people .

question_en: When did the ‘Concorde’ aircraft retired from service?
When did the Allies defeat Germany marking the end of the Second World War?
explanation_en: Following victory on the beaches of Normandy, the allied forces pressed on through France and eventually into Germany. The Allies comprehensively defeated Germany in May 1945 .

question_en: How many square miles does Loch Lomond and the Trossachs National Park cover?
explanation_en: Loch Lomond and the Trossachs National Park covers 720 square miles (1,865 square kilometres) in the west of Scotland.

question_en: Which British athlete won 16 medals, including 11 gold medals, in races over five Paralympic Games?
explanation_en: Baroness Tanni Grey-Thompson (1969-) is an athlete who uses a wheelchair and won 16 Paralympic medals, including 11 gold medals, in races over five Paralympic Games. She won the London Marathon six times and broke a total of 30 world records.

question_en: When is New Year’s Eve celebrated?
explanation_en: New Year’s Eve is celebrated on the 31st of December .

question_en: What is the capital city of Scotland?
explanation_en: The capital city of Scotland is Edinburgh .

question_en: What British sportsmen holds a number of English Test cricket records?
explanation_en: Sir Ian Botham (1955-) captained the English cricket team and holds a number of English Test cricket records, both for batting and for bowling.

question_en: During the Middle Ages, England was an important trading nation and people came to England from abroad to trade and also to work. Where did the weavers come from?
explanation_en: During the Middle Ages, people came to England from abroad to trade and also to work. Many had special skills, such as weavers from France , engineers from Germany, glass manufacturers from Italy and canal builders from Holland.

question_en: What was the name of Sir Francis Drake’s ship and which was one of the first to sail around the world?
explanation_en: Sir Francis Drake, one of the commanders in the defeat of the Spanish Armada, was one of the founders of England’s naval tradition. His ship, the Golden Hind , was one of the first to sail right around (‘circumnavigate’) the world.

question_en: To whom do new citizens have to swear or affirm loyalty as part of the citizenship ceremony?
explanation_en: New citizens swear or affirm loyalty to the King as part of the citizenship ceremony.

question_en: Why did Henry VIII marry Anne of Cleves?
explanation_en: Henry married Anne of Cleves for political reasons but divorced her soon after.

question_en: How many Formula 1 Grand Prix events are held in the UK every year?
explanation_en: A Formula 1 Grand Prix event is held in the UK each year and a number of British Grand Prix drivers have won the Formula 1 World Championship.

question_en: How can you visit the UK Parliament (choose TWO options)?

options.en: Arranging a tour through the visitor services
Writing to your local MP to ask for tickets
Queueing on the day at the public entrance

explanation_en: To visit the UK Parliament, you can write to your local MP in advance to ask for tickets or you can Queue on the day at the public entrance.

question_en: Forced marriage is where one or both parties do not or cannot give their consent to enter into the partnership:
explanation_en: Forced marriage is where one or both parties do not or cannot give their consent to enter into the partnership.

question_en: Northern Ireland and Scotland have their own banknotes, which are valid everywhere in the UK and shops and businesses are obliged to accept them:
explanation_en: Northern Ireland and Scotland have their own banknotes, which are valid everywhere in the UK. However, shops and businesses do not have to accept them .

question_en: How many American colonies declared their independence in 1776, stating that people had a right to establish their own governments?
explanation_en: In 1776, 13 American colonies declared their independence, stating that people had a right to establish their own governments.

question_en: Which TWO words come from the Viking language?
explanation_en: Many of the Viking invaders stayed in Britain – especially in the east and north of England in an area known as the Danelaw (many place names there, such as Grimsby and Scunthorpe come from the Viking languages).

question_en: Which of the following drugs is NOT illegal in the UK?

options.en: All of the above are illegal

explanation_en: Drugs such as heroin, cocaine, ecstasy and cannabis are illegal in the UK.

question_en: How is the Speaker elected?

options.en: By other MPs in a secret ballot
By the members of the House of Lords by proportional representation
By the members of the House of Commons in a public ballot

explanation_en: The Speaker is chosen by other MPs in a secret ballot .

question_en: Which TWO of the following are traditional British foods?
explanation_en: Roast beef and fish and chips are traditional British foods.

question_en: When was the Northern Ireland Parliament established for the first time?
explanation_en: A Northern Ireland Parliament was established in 1922 , when Ireland was divided, but it was abolished in 1972, shortly after the Troubles broke out in 1969.

question_en: Throughout the 1990s, Britain played a leading role in coalition forces involved in the liberation of Kuwait, following the Iraqi invasion in 1990, and the conflict in the former Republic of Yugoslavia:
explanation_en: This statement is true.

question_en: The ‘swinging sixties’ was a period of significant social change and it was known for its growth in:

options.en: British fashion, cinema and popular music

explanation_en: The decade of the 1960s was a period of significant social change. It was known as the ‘swinging sixties’. There was growth in British fashion, cinema and popular music .

question_en: What is the name given to the system used to deal with children and young people who have committed an offence in Scotland?

options.en: The Sheriff Court for Young Children

explanation_en: In Scotland a system called the Children’s Hearings System is used to deal with children and young people who have committed an offence.

question_en: Which prehistoric village located in northern Europe has helped archaeologists to understand more about how people live near the end of the Stone Age?
explanation_en: Skara Brae on Orkney, off the north coast of Scotland, is the best preserved prehistoric village in northern Europe, and has helped archaeologists to understand more about how people lived near the end of the Stone Age.

question_en: During the Middle Ages, a Parliament was developed in Scotland, which had three Houses called Estates. These were:

options.en: The lords, the commons and the clergy
The lords, the commons and the farmers
The farmers, the blacksmiths and the clergy
The farmers, the blacksmiths and the teachers

explanation_en: The Parliament developed in Scotland in the Middle Ages had three Houses, called Estates: the lords, the commons and the clergy .

question_en: Which landscape architect designed grounds around country houses so that the landscape appeared to be natural, with grass, trees and lakes?
explanation_en: In the 18th century, Lancelot ‘Capability’ Brown designed the grounds around country houses so that the landscape appeared to be natural, with grass, trees and lakes. He often worked with Edwin Lutyens to design colourful gardens around the houses he designed.

question_en: Which of the following scientific discoveries helped the progress of the Industrial Revolution?
explanation_en: Scientific discoveries, such as James Watt’s work on steam power , helped the progress of the Industrial Revolution.

question_en: What is the function of the UN Security Council?

options.en: Recommend action when there are international crises and threats to peace
Recommend humanitarian action when there are natural disasters

explanation_en: The UN Security Council, which recommends action when there are international crises and threats to peace .

question_en: Who were the parents of Elizabeth I?

options.en: Henry VII and Anne of Cleves
Henry VIII and Catherine of Aragon

explanation_en: Henry VIII and Anne Boleyn were the parents of Elizabeth I.

question_en: The Isle of Man is a Crown dependency.
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: Which of the following charities works for the prevention of cruelty to children?
explanation_en: The NSPCC is the National Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Children.

question_en: How old do you need to be to buy a lottery ticket or scratch card?
explanation_en: People under 18 are not allowed to participate in the National Lottery.

question_en: Where was Catherine of Aragon (the first wife of Henry VIII) from?
explanation_en: Catherine of Aragon was a Spanish princess .

question_en: When did motor-car racing start in the UK?
explanation_en: Motor-car racing in the UK started in 1902 .

question_en: The Commonwealth is based on the core values of democracy, good government and the rule of law:
explanation_en: The Commonwealth is based on the core values of democracy, good government and the rule of law.

question_en: How long does it take to donate blood?
explanation_en: Giving blood only takes about an hour to do.

question_en: When did the post-war economic boom come to an end?
explanation_en: In the late 1970s , the post-war economic boom came to an end.

question_en: During its government, Margaret Thatcher made structural changes to the economy through the privatisation of nationalised industries and imposed legal controls on trade union powers:
explanation_en: Margaret Thatcher made structural changes to the economy through the privatisation of nationalised industries and imposed legal controls on trade union powers.

question_en: Who sat at the House of Commons during the Middle Ages?

options.en: Knights and wealthy people from towns and cities
Everyone could sit at the House of Commons

explanation_en: In the Middle Ages, Knights, who were usually smaller landowners, and wealthy people from towns and cities were elected to sit in the House of Commons.

question_en: Which of the following words come from the Anglo-Saxon language?
explanation_en: The words ‘Apple’ and ‘summer’ are based on Anglo-Saxon words.

question_en: Where do local authorities get funding from (choose TWO answers)?
explanation_en: Local authorities provide a range of services in their areas. They are funded by money from central government and by local taxes .

question_en: If you are self-employed:

options.en: You need to pay your own tax
Tax is automatically taken from your earnings by a specialised agency
You don’t need to pay tax
You only needed to pay tax on 50% of your earnings

explanation_en: If you are self-employed, you need to pay your own tax through a system called ‘self-assessment’, which includes completing a tax return.

question_en: What name is given to the elected members of the Northern Ireland Assembly?
explanation_en: Members of the Northern Ireland Assembly are known as MLAs (members of the Legislative Assembly).

question_en: How long does the Hanukkah celebration last for?
explanation_en: Hanukkah is in November or December and is celebrated for eight days .

question_en: Which of the following areas did the British Empire NOT cover during the Victorian period?
explanation_en: During the Victorian period, the British Empire grew to cover all of India, Australia and large parts of Africa.

question_en: What percentage of the total British population is located in Wales?
explanation_en: The total British population located in Wales is around 5% .

question_en: Which senior MP is responsible for issues relating to crime, policing and immigration?
explanation_en: The Home Secretary is responsible for crime, policing and immigration.

question_en: Which British scientist led the development of the atomic bomb?
explanation_en: Scientists led by Ernest Rutherford , working at Manchester and then Cambridge University, were the first to ‘split the atom’ and took part in the Manhattan Project in the United States, which developed the atomic bomb.

question_en: Which of the following movies played an important role in boosting morale during the Second World War?
explanation_en: During the Second World War, British movies (for example, In Which We Serve ) played an important part in boosting morale.

question_en: After the Black Death, the growing wealth in the towns led to the development of a strong middle class:
explanation_en: After the Black Death, new social classes appeared, including owners of large areas of land (later called the gentry), and people left the countryside to live in the towns. In the towns, growing wealth led to the development of a strong middle class.

question_en: What was the ability of the Harrier jump jet aircraft?
explanation_en: The Harrier jump jet, an aircraft capable of taking off vertically , was also designed and developed in the UK.

question_en: Who won the 2012 Olympic gold medal in heptathlon?
explanation_en: Jessica Ennis (1986-) won the 2012 Olympic gold medal in the heptathlon, which includes seven different track and field events. She also holds a number of British athletics records.

question_en: What is the name of the annual cultural Welsh festival which includes music, dance, art and original performances largely in Welsh?

options.en: The National Performance Festival of Wales

explanation_en: The National Eisteddfod of Wales is an annual cultural festival which includes music, dance, art and original performances largely in Welsh. It includes a number of important competitions for Welsh poetry.

question_en: What is the most well-known rugby league (club) competition?
explanation_en: The Super League is the most well-known rugby league (club) competition.

question_en: Where do the Laurence Olivier Awards take place?
explanation_en: The Laurence Olivier Awards take place annually at different venues in London .

question_en: How old was the Queen of Scotland, Mary Stuart, when she became queen?
explanation_en: The queen of Scotland, Mary Stuart (often now called ‘Mary, Queen of Scots’) was a Catholic. She was only a week old when her father died and she became queen.

question_en: Where was the first tennis club founded?
explanation_en: The first tennis club was founded in Leamington Spa in 1872.

question_en: Which British playwright invented many common English words and had a great influence on the English language?
explanation_en: William Shakespeare had a great influence on the English language and invented many words that are still common today.

question_en: In which city is the Northern Ireland Assembly building, also known as Stormont, located?
explanation_en: The Northern Ireland Assembly building is known as Stormont and it is located in Belfast .

question_en: What proportion of the population died as a result of the Black Death in England?
explanation_en: As a result of the Black Death, one third of the population of England died and a similar proportion in Scotland and Wales.

question_en: Which famous poem tells the story of the knights at the court of King Arthur?

options.en: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight

explanation_en: Poems which survive from the Middle Ages include Chaucer’s Canterbury Tales and a poem called Sir Gawain and the Green Knight , about one of the knights at the court of King Arthur.

question_en: Which of the following is NOT a fundamental principle of British life?

options.en: Tolerance of those with different faiths and beliefs

explanation_en: The fundamental principles of British life include: tolerance of those with different faiths and beliefs, the rule of law, democracy, individual liberty and participation in community life.

question_en: Which flower is associated with Northern Ireland?
explanation_en: The shamrock is the flower associated with Northern Ireland.

question_en: The longest distance on the mainland in the UK is from John O’Groats on the north coast of Scotland to a place in the south-west corner of England known as:
explanation_en: The longest distance on the mainland is from John O’Groats on the north coast of Scotland to Land’s End in the south-west corner of England. It is about 870 miles (approximately 1,400 kilometres).

question_en: Who wrote a famous series of poems about a group of people going to Canterbury on a pilgrimage known as ‘The Canterbury Tales’?
explanation_en: In the years leading up to 1400, Geoffrey Chaucer wrote a series of poems in English about a group of people going to Canterbury on a pilgrimage. The people decided to tell each other stories on the journey, and the poems describe the travellers and some of the stories they told. This collection of poems is called The Canterbury Tales.

question_en: Which of the following categories is recognised at the Laurence Olivier Awards?
explanation_en: The Laurence Olivier Awards recognise achievements in the area of theatre .

question_en: In the UK, there is a National Lottery for which draws are made every day.
explanation_en: In the UK, there is a National Lottery for which draws are made every week .

question_en: Where is Europe’s longest dry ski slope located?
explanation_en: There are five ski centres in Scotland, as well as Europe’s longest dry ski slope near Edinburgh .

question_en: Which TWO of Henry VIII’s wives were accused of taking lovers and executed?
explanation_en: Anne Boleyn and Catherine Howard were accused of taking lovers and executed.

question_en: Who is the head of the state in the UK?

options.en: Prince Philip, the Duke of Edinburgh

explanation_en: King Charles III is the head of state in the UK.

question_en: What was the population of the UK in 1600?
explanation_en: The population of the UK in 1600 was just over 4 million people .

question_en: What name is given to the 31st of December in Scotland?
explanation_en: In Scotland, 31 December is called Hogmanay .

question_en: In which of the following languages is the life in the UK test NOT available?
explanation_en: The Life in the UK test is usually taken is English, although special arrangements can be made if you wish to take it in Welsh or Scottish Gaelic.

question_en: Which two scientists were successful at cloning the first mammal, Dolly the sheep?

options.en: Sir Frank Whittle and Sir Christopher Cockrell
Sir Robert Edwards and Patrick Steptoe
Sir Ian Wilmot and Keith Campbell
Francis Creek and John Logie Baird

explanation_en: In 1996, two British scientists, Sir Ian Wilmot and Keith Campbell , led a team which was the first to succeed in cloning a mammal, Dolly the sheep.

question_en: When is the Sikh Vaisakhi festival celebrated?
explanation_en: Vaisakhi (also spelled Baisakhi) is a Sikh festival which celebrates the founding of the Sikh community known as the Khalsa. It is celebrated on the 14th of April each year with parades, dancing and singing.

question_en: Who were the Pre-Raphaelites?

options.en: An important group of musicians from the 16th century
An important group who demanded the vote for the working classes
An important group of artists in the second half of the 19th century
A group of explorers who arrived in the south-east coast of Australia

explanation_en: The Pre-Raphaelites were an important group of artists in the second half of the 19th century . They painted detailed pictures on religious or literary themes in bright colours. The group included Holman Hunt, Dante Gabriel Rossetti and Sir John Millais.

question_en: When was St Pancras Station built?
explanation_en: St Pancras Station was built in the 19th century .

question_en: Members of the Scottish Parliament (MSPs) are elected on the basis of:

options.en: First past the post system (the candidate who gets the most votes)

explanation_en: Members of the Scottish Parliament (MSPs) are elected by a form of proportional representation .

question_en: Which TWO of the following soap operas are popular in the UK?
explanation_en: Popular programmes in the UK include regular soap operas such as Coronation Street and EastEnders .

question_en: What does the term ‘Hansard’ relate to?

options.en: Official reports where proceedings in Parliament are published
A festival which celebrates the founding of the Sikh community

explanation_en: Proceedings in Parliament are broadcast on television and published in official reports called Hansard.

question_en: Who was the first king of the House of Tudor?
explanation_en: Henry VII (Henry Tudor) was the first king of the House of Tudor.

question_en: What do Lewis Hamilton, Jenson Button and Damon Hill have in common?

options.en: They have won the Formula 1 World Championship
They have won the Six Nations Championship

explanation_en: Lewis Hamilton, Jenson Button and Damon Hill have won the Formula 1 World Championship.

question_en: According to the 2011 Census, what percentage of people said that they had no religion?
explanation_en: According to the 2011 census, 25% of people said they had no religion.

question_en: During which period were the first coins produced in Britain?
explanation_en: The people of the Iron Age had a sophisticated culture and economy. They made the first coins to be minted in Britain, some inscribed with the names of Iron Age kings.

question_en: What other name is given to Diwali, the religious celebration that celebrates the victory of good over evil and the gaining of knowledge?
explanation_en: Diwali normally falls in October or November and lasts for five days. It is often called the Festival of Lights .

question_en: In Wales, the jury has to listen to the evidence presented at the trial and then decide a verdict of ‘guilty’, ‘not guilty’ or ‘not proven’ based of what they have heard:
explanation_en: The jury has to listen to the evidence presented at the trial and then decide a verdict of ‘guilty’ or ‘not guilty’ based of what they have heard. In Scotland, a third verdict of ‘not proven’ is also possible.

question_en: The Prime Minister has a country house outside London called:
explanation_en: The Prime Minister has a country house outside London called Chequers .

question_en: In which English county is Maiden Castle located?
explanation_en: A very impressive hill fort can be seen today at Maiden Castle, in the English county of Dorset .

question_en: A National Insurance number does on its own prove to an employer that you have the right to work in the UK:
explanation_en: A National Insurance number does not on its own prove to an employer that you have the right to work in the UK.

question_en: Which of the following is a ceremonial role to be undertaken by the King?

options.en: Opening the new parliamentary session every year
Visiting the major cities in the UK once every six months
Giving a speech on New Year’s Eve

explanation_en: The King has important ceremonial roles, such as the opening of the new parliamentary session each year . On this occasion the King makes a speech which summarises the government’s policies for the year ahead.

question_en: How long can you use your driving licence in the UK if you have a licence from another country?

options.en: You need a UK driving licence
For as long as it is valid

explanation_en: If you have a licence from another country, you may use it in the UK for up to 12 months .

question_en: What British discovery was influential in the development of computer science and the modern-day computer?   The Radar
explanation_en: The Turing machine is a theoretical mathematical device invented by Alan Turing (1912-54), a British mathematician, in the 1930s. The theory was influential in the development of computer science and the modern-day computer.

question_en: Which Court deals with the serious criminal offences in England, Wales and Northern Ireland?
explanation_en: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland, serious criminal offences are tried in front of a judge and a jury in a Crown Court .

question_en: Which famous philosopher developed ideas about human nature during the 18th century and that have continued to influence philosophers over the years?
explanation_en: During the 18th century, David Hume ’s ideas about human nature continue to influence philosophers.

question_en: If you are arrested and taken to a police station, a police officer should:

options.en: Make a copy of your ID and retain you until your lawyer arrives
Tell you the reason for your arrest and that you will be able to seek legal advice
Offer you a cup of tea
Explain the law to you and wait for your family to arrive

explanation_en: If you are arrested and taken to a police station, a police officer will tell you the reason for your arrest and you will be able to seek legal advice .

question_en: Which British actor has recently won an Oscar?
explanation_en: Recent British actors to have won Oscars include Colin Firth, Sir Antony Hopkins , Dame Judi Dench, Kate Winslet and Tilda Swinton.

question_en: What TWO names are given to the day before Lent?
explanation_en: The day before Lent starts is called Shrove Tuesday, or Pancake Day .

question_en: When did the ‘Concorde’ aircraft carry passengers for the first time?
explanation_en: The ‘Concorde’ began carrying passengers in 1976 .

question_en: The UK is one of five permanent members of the Security Council:
explanation_en: The UK is one of five permanent members of the Security Council.

question_en: Which of the following landmarks is the largest expanse of fresh water in mainland Britain?
explanation_en: Loch Lomond is the largest expanse of fresh water in mainland Britain and probably the best-known part of the park.

question_en: Who wrote the music for the popular show Jesus Christ Superstar?

options.en: Hans Holbein and Sir Anthony Van Dyck

explanation_en: Andrew Lloyd Webber has written the music for shows which have been popular throughout the world, including, in collaboration with Tim Rice, Jesus Christ Superstar and Evita, and also Cats and The Phantom of the Opera.

question_en: Which one of the following four changes did the Chartists did NOT campaign for?

options.en: For any man to be able to stand as MP

explanation_en: The Chartists campaigned for every man to have the vote, elections every year, for all regions to be equal in the electoral system, secret ballots, for any man to be able to stand as an MP and for MPs to be paid.

question_en: Pantomimes are a British tradition that many theatres produce during:
explanation_en: Many theatres produce a pantomime at Christmas time .

question_en: How many weeks does the orchestral classical music event known as ‘the Proms’ last for?
explanation_en: The Proms is an eight-week summer season of orchestral classical music that takes place in various venues, including the Royal Albert Hall in London.

question_en: When did King Charles III start reining?
explanation_en: The King has reigned since his mother’s death in September 2022 .

question_en: What did the Chartists campaign for?

options.en: The right to vote for women over 30 years old
The right to vote for the working classes and other people without property
The right to vote for women and men over 30 years old
The right to vote for every citizen

explanation_en: The Chartists, demanded the vote for the working classes and other people without property .

question_en: What is the name of the land formation consisting of columns made of volcanic lava located on the north-east coast of Northern Ireland?
explanation_en: Located on the north-east coast of Northern Ireland, the Giant’s Causeway is a land formation of columns made from volcanic lava.

question_en: Members of the public are not allowed in Youth Courts, and the name or photographs of the accused young person cannot be published in newspapers or used by the media:
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: A set of principles by which a country is governed and which includes all of the institutions that are responsible for running the country and how their power is kept in check is the definition for:
explanation_en: A constitution is a set of principles by which a country is governed. It includes all of the institutions that are responsible for running the country and how their power is kept in check.

question_en: When did Emmeline Pankhurst die?
explanation_en: Emmeline Pankhurst died in 1928 . Shortly before Emmeline’s death, women were given the right to vote at the age of 21, the same as men.

question_en: What TWO houses were confronted during the Wars of the Roses?
explanation_en: The Wars of the Roses were fought between the supporters of two families: the House of Lancaster and the House of York .

question_en: What is the National Anthem of the UK?
explanation_en: The National Anthem of the UK is ‘God Save the King’ .

question_en: Who was the first woman to become Prime Minister in the UK?
explanation_en: Margaret Thatcher , Britain’s first woman Prime Minister, led the Conservative government from 1979 to 1990.

question_en: When did the Brexit referendum take place?
explanation_en: The referendum on the UK’s membership of the European Union or Brexit referendum was held on 23 June 2016 .

question_en: Which one of the following gardens is located in Northern Ireland?
explanation_en: Mount Stewart is located in Northern Ireland, Bodnant Garden in Wales and Sissinghurst and Hidcote in England.

question_en: When is the Mercury Music Prize awarded?
explanation_en: The Mercury Music Prize is awarded each September for the best album from the UK and Ireland.

question_en: Which of the following statements is true?

options.en: To apply to become a permanent resident or citizen of the UK, you will need to have a university degree
To apply to become a permanent resident or citizen of the UK, you will need to have a good understanding of life in the UK
To apply to become a permanent resident or citizen of the UK, you will need to have British relatives
To apply to become a permanent resident or citizen of the UK, you will need to have knowledge of the British laws

explanation_en: To apply to become a permanent resident or citizen of the UK, you will need to have a good understanding of life in the UK and be able to speak and read English.

question_en: Who invented the cash-dispensing ATM (automatic teller machine) or ‘cashpoint’?
explanation_en: In the 1960s, James Goodfellow (1937-) invented the cash-dispensing ATM (automatic teller machine) or ‘cashpoint’. The first of these was put into use by Barclays Bank in Enfield, north London in 1967.

question_en: What is the name of the only ‘Grand Slam’ tennis event played on grass?
explanation_en: The Wimbledon Championships is the oldest tennis tournament in the world and the only ‘Grand Slam’ event played on grass.

question_en: What is the meaning of the term ‘Habeas corpus’?

options.en: You must present the person in court

explanation_en: Habeas corpus is Latin for ‘you must present the person in court’. The Habeas Corpus Act guaranteed that no one could be held prisoner unlawfully. Every prisoner has a right to a court hearing.

question_en: What name is given to the candelabrum lit during the eight-day holiday of Hanukkah?
explanation_en: Hanukkah is in November or December and is celebrated for eight days. On each day of the festival a candle is lit on a stand of eight candles (called a menorah ) to remember the story of the festival, where oil that should have lasted only a day did so for eight.

question_en: Which of the following statements is TRUE?

options.en: It is against the law to walk your dog on the beach
It is against the law to treat a pet cruelly or to neglect it
It is against the law to take pets into public places
It is against the law to feed your pet in public places

explanation_en: It is against the law to treat a pet cruelly or to neglect it.

question_en: Which German princess got married with Henry VIII?
explanation_en: Anne of Cleves was a German princess who married Henry VIII for political reasons.

question_en: In which of the following matters can the Scottish Parliament legislate (choose FIVE options)?
explanation_en: The Scottish Parliament can legislate on: civil and criminal law, health, education, planning and additional tax-raising powers .

question_en: Drinking in public can be a criminal offence and you can be fined or arrested for it:
explanation_en: Drinking in public: some places have alcohol-free zones where you cannot drink in public. The police can also confiscate alcohol or move young people on from public places. You can be fined or arrested.

question_en: What was the most spoken language during the Iron Age?
explanation_en: The language spoken during the Iron Age was part of the Celtic language family.

question_en: What charity works for the preservation of important buildings, coastline and countryside in the UK?
explanation_en: The National Trust works for the preservation of important buildings, coastline and countryside in the UK.

question_en: When did Henry VIII die?
explanation_en: Henry VIII died on the 28th of January 1547 .

question_en: Which of the following lines from Shakespeare’s plays and poems is often still quoted?

options.en: I have nothing to offer but blood, toil, tears and sweat

explanation_en: ‘The darling buds of May’ is a line from William Shakespeare’s play Sonnet 18 – Shall I Compare Thee To A Summer’s Day.

question_en: Which TWO of the following are fundamental principles of British life?

options.en: Tolerance of those with different faiths and beliefs

explanation_en: Tolerance of those with different faiths and beliefs and the rule of law are fundamental principles of British life.

question_en: What is the capital city of the UK?
explanation_en: The capital city of the UK is London .

question_en: Which of the following statements about Mary, Queen of Scotland, is NOT true?

options.en: She spent most of her childhood in France
She gave her throne to her Protestant son, James VI of Scotland.
She was kept her prisoner for 20 years

explanation_en: The queen of Scotland, Mary Stuart (often now called ‘Mary, Queen of Scots’) was a Catholic.

question_en: When did Ireland become unified with England, Wales and Scotland?
explanation_en: In 1801 , Ireland became unified with England, Scotland and Wales after the Act of Union of 1800. This created the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland.

question_en: What is Hogmanay?

options.en: The equivalent of Christmas Day in Scotland
The equivalent of New Year’s Eve in Scotland
The equivalent of Christmas Day in Wales
The equivalent of New Year’s Eve in Wales

explanation_en: In Scotland, the 31st of December is called Hogmanay.

question_en: The devolved administrations of Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland each have their own civil service:
explanation_en: The devolved administrations each have their own civil service.

question_en: On Remembrance Day, there is a two-minute silence at 11 AM to commemorate those who died fighting during the First World War.
explanation_en: On Remembrance Day at 11.00 am there is a two-minute silence and wreaths are laid at the Cenotaph in Whitehall, London.

question_en: What is the distance between the north coast of Scotland and the south-west corner of England?
explanation_en: In the UK, the longest distance on the mainland is from John O’Groats on the north coast of Scotland to Land’s End in the south-west corner of England. It is about 870 miles (approximately 1,400 kilometres).

question_en: Who was Henry Purcell?
explanation_en: Henry Purcell (1659-95) was the organist at Westminster Abbey . He wrote church music, operas and other pieces, and developed a British style distinct from that elsewhere in Europe.

question_en: How are life peers appointed?

options.en: By the monarch on the advice of the Prime Minister
By the Prime Minister on the advice of the King

explanation_en: Life peers are appointed by the monarch on the advice of the Prime Minister .

question_en: Where is the Giant’s Causeway located?
explanation_en: The Giant’s Causeway is located on the north-east coast of Northern Ireland .

question_en: Who proposed that enemy aircrafts could be detected by radio waves and is responsible for the development of the radar?
explanation_en: The radar was developed by Scotsman Sir Robert Watson-Watt (1892-1973), who proposed that enemy aircraft could be detected by radio waves. The first successful radar test took place in 1935.

question_en: During Halloween people carve lanterns out of ______ and put a candle inside of them.
explanation_en: During Halloween people carve lanterns out of pumpkins and put a candle inside of them.

question_en: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland, if an accused person is aged 18, the case will be heard in a Youth Court:
explanation_en: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland, if an accused person is aged 10 to 17 , the case is normally heard in a Youth Court in front of up to three specially trained magistrates or a District Judge.

question_en: The Prime Minister appoints about 40 senior MPs to become ministers in charge of departments.
explanation_en: The Prime Minister appoints about 20 senior MPs to become ministers in charge of departments.

question_en: Royal Ascot is a four-day horse race meeting in Berkshire attended by members of the Royal Family:
explanation_en: Royal Ascot, a five-day race meeting in Berkshire attended by members of the Royal Family.

question_en: Who sat at the House of Lords during the Middle Ages?

options.en: Knights, who were usually smaller landowners
Wealthy people from towns and cities

explanation_en: During the Middle Ages, great landowners and bishops sat in the House of Lords.

question_en: Which of the following statements regarding Scottish television is TRUE?

options.en: There is a channel with programmes in the Gaelic language
There are three channels in the Gaelic language
All programmes are broadcasted in English
There are no specific programmes to Scotland

explanation_en: In Scotland, some Scotland-specific programmes are shown and there is also a channel with programmes in the Gaelic language .

question_en: Where does the biggest and most well-known Fringe Festival take place?
explanation_en: The biggest and most well-known Fringe Festival is the Edinburgh Festival Fringe .

question_en: The UN was set up after the First World War and aims to prevent war and promote international peace and security:
explanation_en: The UN was set up after the Second World War and aims to prevent war and promote international peace and security.

question_en: Which of the following countries was NOT part of the Central Powers during the First World War?
explanation_en: During the First World War Serbia formed part of the Allied powers .

question_en: What is played at important national occasions and at events attended by the King or the Royal Family?
explanation_en: The National Anthem of the UK is ‘God Save the King’ is played at important national occasions and at events attended by the King or the Royal Family.

question_en: For which purpose was the Statute of Rhuddlan introduced?

options.en: To annex Scotland to the Crown of England
To annex Northern Ireland to the Crown of England
To annex Ireland to the Crown of England
To annex Wales to the Crown of England

explanation_en: In 1284 King Edward I of England introduced the Statute of Rhuddlan, which annexed Wales to the Crown of England .

question_en: How can a person be protected from being forced into a marriage?

options.en: By giving them a place to stay away from their family
Court orders can be obtained to protect a person from being forced into a marriage
By speaking to the person’s parents
There are no laws in the UK to avoid this

explanation_en: Court orders can be obtained to protect a person from being forced into a marriage, or to protect a person in a forced marriage.

question_en: When was the first tennis club founded in the UK?
explanation_en: The first tennis club was founded in Leamington Spa in 1872 .

question_en: Which of the following is NOT classified as a criminal offence in the UK?

options.en: Unfair dismissal or discrimination in the workplace

explanation_en: Unfair dismissal or discrimination in the workplace is classified as a civil offence.

question_en: When did Henry VIII start reining?
explanation_en: Henry VIII was king of England from 21 April 1509 until his death on 28 January 1547.

question_en: What is the currency in the UK?
explanation_en: The currency in the UK is the pound sterling (symbol £).

question_en: In May 2010, the Conservative and Liberal Democrat parties formed a coalition and the leader of the Liberal Democrat Party, David Cameron, became Prime Minister:
explanation_en: In May 2010, and for the first time in the UK since February 1974, no political party won an overall majority in the General Election. The Conservative and Liberal Democrat parties formed a coalition and the leader of the Conservative Party, David Cameron, became Prime Minister.

question_en: When is Diwali normally celebrated?
explanation_en: Diwali normally falls in October or November and lasts for five days.

question_en: Which of the following is NOT considered to be a main part of the British government?
explanation_en: The European Council is not part of the British government.

question_en: Which TWO universities compete with each other on a rowing race along the River Thames every year?
explanation_en: There is a popular yearly rowing race on the Thames between Oxford and Cambridge Universities .

question_en: What is the name of Admiral Nelson’s ship?
explanation_en: Admiral Nelson’s ship was known as the HMS Victory .

question_en: When driving in the UK, where do you need to display the tax disc which shows that the annual road tax has been paid?
explanation_en: When driving in the UK, you must pay an annual road tax and display the tax disc, which shows that the tax has been paid, on the windowscreen .

question_en: What is the capital of the UK?
explanation_en: The capital city of the UK is London .

question_en: Which of the following is NOT a British film?
explanation_en: The Lord of the Rings is not the British film.

question_en: Which of the following statements is TRUE?

options.en: A jury is made up of members of the public who have applied to be part of it
A jury is made up of members of the public chosen at random from the local electoral register

explanation_en: A jury is made up of members of the public chosen at random from the local electoral register.

question_en: When is St David’s Day, patron of Wales?
explanation_en: St David’s Day, patron of Wales is celebrated on the 1st of March .

question_en: What name is given to the red flowers found on the battlefields of the First World War and which people wear during Remembrance Day?
explanation_en: Remembrance Day people wear poppies (the red flower found on the battlefields of the First World War).

question_en: It is a civil offence to cause harassment, alarm or distress to someone because of their religion or ethnic origin:
explanation_en: It is a criminal offence to cause harassment, alarm or distress to someone because of their religion or ethnic origin.

question_en: Where can you get contact details for all your representatives and their parties (choose TWO options)?
explanation_en: You can get contact details for all your representatives and their parties from your local library and from http://www.parliament.uk .

question_en: What is the Old Bailey?

options.en: The name of a traditional Welsh cake

explanation_en: The Old Bailey is probably the most famous criminal court in the world .

question_en: What is the name of the oldest continuously working film studio facility in the world?
explanation_en: Ealing Studios has a claim to being the oldest continuously working film studio facility in the world.

question_en: What are the British scientists Sir Ian Wilmot and Keith Campbell famous for?

options.en: The cloning of the first mammal, Dolly the sheep
The invention of the MRI (magnetic resonance imaging) scanner
The invention of the World Wide Web

explanation_en: Sir Ian Wilmot and Keith Campbell led a team which was the first to succeed in cloning a mammal, Dolly the sheep .

question_en: By which name is London’s west end known for?
explanation_en: London’s west end is also known as ‘Theatreland’ .

question_en: Which of the following was one of the commanders in the defeat of the Spanish Armada and one of the founders of England’s naval tradition?
explanation_en: Sir Francis Drake was one of the commanders in the defeat of the Spanish Armada and one of the founders of England’s naval tradition.

question_en: Where was William Shakespeare born?
explanation_en: William Shakespeare was born in Stratford-upon-Avon .

question_en: Which TWO islands are closely linked to the UK but are not part of it?
explanation_en: The Channel Islands and the Isle of Man are closely linked to the UK but are not part of it.

question_en: Who gave Henry VIII the son he wanted, Edward?
explanation_en: Jane Seymour gave Henry VIII the son he wanted, Edward.

question_en: The Middle Ages or medieval times relate to the time that goes from the period after the Norman Conquest until the year:
explanation_en: The period after the Norman Conquest up until about 1485 is called the Middle Ages (or the medieval period).

question_en: Who is the heir to the throne of the UK?
explanation_en: The King’s eldest son, Prince William (the Prince of Wales) , is the heir to the throne.

question_en: Which flower is associated with Wales?
explanation_en: The daffodil is the flower associated with Wales.

question_en: What was the nickname given to the Scottish poet Robert Burns?
explanation_en: Robert Burns, a Scottish poet, was Known in Scotland as ‘The Bard’ .

question_en: When was Ireland divided into two countries?
explanation_en: In 1921 a peace treaty was signed and in 1922 Ireland became two countries.

question_en: Which of the following is NOT a city of the UK?
explanation_en: Dublin is not a city of the UK.

question_en: When is Bonfire Night celebrated?
explanation_en: Bonfire Night is celebrated on the 5th of November .

question_en: What is the name of the event that refers to the 6 June 1944 when Allied Forces landed in Normandy aiming to attack Hitler’s forces in Western Europe during World War II?
explanation_en: ‘D-Day’ refers to the 6th of June 1944, when allied forces landed in Normandy aiming to attack Hitler’s forces in Western Europe during World War II.

question_en: In the UK, many people continue to visit the countryside for holidays and for leisure activities such as  walking, camping and fishing:
explanation_en: Many people continue to visit the countryside for holidays and for leisure activities such as  walking, camping and fishing.

question_en: What is the name of the building where the Northern Ireland Assembly meet?
explanation_en: In Northern Ireland elected members, known as MLAs, meet in the Northern Ireland Assembly at Stormont, in Belfast .

question_en: Which British Prime Minister led the Conservative government from 1979 to 1990?
explanation_en: Margaret Thatcher , Britain’s first woman Prime Minister, led the Conservative government from 1979 to 1990.

question_en: Which of the following civilisations is known for having built roads and public buildings, creating a structure of law and having introduced new plants and animals in Britain?
