explanation_en: The Romans remained in Britain for 400 years. They built roads and public buildings, created a structure of law, and introduced new plants and animals.

question_en: How often does the Edinburgh Festival take place?
explanation_en: The Edinburgh Festival takes place in Edinburgh, Scotland, every summer .

question_en: Which of the following countries is NOT a member of the Commonwealth?
explanation_en: Zimbabwe is not a member of the Commonwealth.

question_en: What marked the beginning of what is called ‘constitutional monarchy’?

options.en: The laws passed after the Glorious Revolution
A speech given by the King
The emergence of new ideas about politics, philosophy and science
The development of the Bessemer process

explanation_en: The laws passed after the Glorious Revolution are the beginning of what is called ‘constitutional monarchy’.

question_en: What name is given to the day that marks the end of the Ramadan, when Muslims have fasted for a month?
explanation_en: Eid a-Fitr celebrates the end of Ramadan, when Muslims have fasted for a month.

question_en: Who is the fastest person to have sailed around the world single-handed?
explanation_en: Dame Ellen MacArthur is a yachtswoman and in 2004 became the fastest person to sail around the world single-handed.

question_en: Who makes the decisions on government policies in the UK?

options.en: Only the Prime Minister can make the decisions on government policies
The Prime Minister and the Cabinet
The monarch and the Prime Minister

explanation_en: Decisions on government policies are made by the Prime Minister and cabinet .

question_en: Where does the most famous sailing event in the UK take place?
explanation_en: The most famous sailing event in the UK takes place at Cowes on the Isle of Wight .

question_en: How often is the electoral register updated?
explanation_en: The electoral register is updated every year in September or October .

question_en: What do you need to drive a car in the UK?

options.en: A driving licence, a valid motor insurance, a MOT certificate and the road tax
A driving licence and a valid motor insurance only
A driving licence and a MOT certificate only

explanation_en: To drive a car in the UK you need a driving licence, a valid motor insurance, a MOT certificate (for cars over three years old) and the road tax.

question_en: What is the capital city of Northern Ireland?
explanation_en: The capital city of Northern Ireland is Belfast .

question_en: What name is given to the day when people play jokes on each other until midday?
explanation_en: April Fool’s Day , 1 April, is a day when people play jokes on each other until midday. The television and newspapers often have stories that are April Fool jokes.

question_en: Who is the fastest person to have sailed around the world single-handed?
explanation_en: Dame Ellen MacArthur (1976-) is a yachtswoman and in 2004 became the fastest person to sail around the world singlehanded.

question_en: When did the First World War end?
explanation_en: The First World War ended at 11.00 am on the 11th November 1918 with victory for Britain and its allies.

question_en: What are Welsh cakes made of?

options.en: Bacon, eggs, sausage, black pudding and potato bread

explanation_en: Welsh cakes are a traditional Welsh snack made from flour, dried fruits and spices , and served either hot or cold.

question_en: Where is the London Eye situated?

options.en: On the northern bank of the River Thames
On the southern bank of the River Thames
On the northern bank of the River Lea
On the southern bank of the River Lea

explanation_en: The London Eye is situated on the southern bank of the River Thames and is a Ferris wheel that is 443 feet (135 metres) tall.

question_en: What is the relationship between the monarch and the government?

options.en: Approve the government’s decisions on government policies
Advise, warn and encourage the government’s decisions
Advise, encourage and approve the government’s decisions
Advise and approve the government’s decisions

explanation_en: The monarch has regular meetings with the Prime Minister and can advise, warn and encourage, but the decisions on government policies are made by the Prime Minister and cabinet.

question_en: The UK is a ‘constitutional monarchy’:
explanation_en: The UK has a constitutional monarchy.

question_en: What do you need to apply to become a permanent resident or citizen of the UK (Choose TWO options)?

options.en: To be able to speak and read English
A good understanding of life in the UK

explanation_en: To apply to become a permanent resident or citizen of the UK, you will need to speak and read English and have a good understanding of life in the UK.

question_en: Which British scientist was awarded a Nobel Prize for discovering the structure of the DNA molecule?
explanation_en: The structure of the DNA molecule was discovered in 1953 through work at British universities in London and Cambridge. This discovery contributed to many scientific advances, particularly in medicine and fighting crime. Francis Crick (1916-2004), one of those awarded the Nobel Prize for this discovery, was British.

question_en: Who established the Church of England?
explanation_en: To divorce his first wife, Henry VIII needed the approval of the Pope. When the Pope refused, Henry established the Church of England. In this new church, the king, not the Pope, would have the power to appoint bishops and order how people should worship.

question_en: When does Lent take place?
explanation_en: The 40 days before Easter are known as Lent. It is a time when Christians take time to reflect and prepare for Easter.

question_en: Which of the following national galleries is NOT located in London?
explanation_en: The National Museum is located in Cardiff, Wales.

question_en: How much can you be fined if you watch TV but do not have a TV licence?
explanation_en: You will receive a fine up to £1,000 if you watch TV but do not have a TV licence.

question_en: How old is the clock at the Houses of Parliament in London, also known as ‘Big Ben’?
explanation_en: Big Ben is the nickname for the great bell of the clock at the House of Parliament in London. The clock is over 150 years old and is a popular tourist attraction.

question_en: The UK was a full member of the European Union but did not use the Euro currency.
explanation_en: This statement is true.

question_en: The North Atlantic Treaty Organisation is a group of European and North American countries that was created with which TWO purposes:

options.en: Protect and promote human rights in those countries
Help each other if they come under attack
Maintain peace between all its members
Promote free trade amongst those countries

explanation_en: NATO is a group of European and North American countries that have agreed to help each other if they come under attack. It also aims to maintain peace between all of its members.

question_en: The small claims procedure is used in England and Wales for minor disputes for:
explanation_en: This procedure is used for claims of less than £10,000 in England and Wales and £3,000 in Scotland and Northern Ireland.

question_en: What name is given to the period in the 18th century when new ideas about politics, philosophy and science were developed?
explanation_en: During the 18th century, new ideas about politics, philosophy and science were developed. This is often called ‘ the Enlightenment ’.

question_en: What is the meaning of ‘Magna Carta’?
explanation_en: The Magna Carta (which means the Great Charter ). The Magna Carta established the idea that even the king was subject to the law. It protected the rights of the nobility and restricted the king’s power to collect taxes or to make and change laws.

question_en: When did the Wars of the Roses end?
explanation_en: The Wars of the Roses ended with the Battle of Bosworth Field in 1485.

question_en: Which of the following is the responsibility of the Chancellor of the Exchequer?
explanation_en: The Chancellor of the Exchequer is responsible for the economy.

question_en: In which British university did Isaac Newton study?
explanation_en: Isaac Newton studied at Cambridge University .

question_en: Who invaded England after the Romans?
explanation_en: The Roman army left Britain in AD 410 to defend other parts of the Roman Empire and never returned. Britain was again invaded by tribes from northern Europe: the Jutes, the Angles and the Saxons.

question_en: Who was the first person to lead a Roman invasion in Britain in 55 BC?
explanation_en: Julius Caesar led a Roman invasion of Britain in 55 BC.

question_en: Which name is given to the tombs where people buried their dead during the Bronze Age?
explanation_en: During the Bronze Age people lived in roundhouses and buried their dead in tombs called round barrows .

question_en: What did the ‘Bill of Rights‘ confirm in 1689?

options.en: The rights of Parliament and the limits of the king‘s power
The supreme power of the king

explanation_en: The Bill of Rights, 1689, confirmed the rights of Parliament and the limits of the king’s power . Parliament took control of who could be monarch and declared that the king or queen must be a Protestant.

question_en: Who designed the Clifton Suspension Bridge located over the Avon George?
explanation_en: The Clifton Suspension Bridge was designed by Isambard Kingdom Brunel and is spanning the Avon Gorge.

question_en: During the Middle Ages, England was an important trading nation and people came to England from abroad to trade and also to work. Where did the glass manufacturers come from?
Which American President worked closely with Margaret Thatcher?
explanation_en: Margaret Thatcher worked closely with the United States President, Ronald Reagan , and was one of the first Western leaders to recognise and welcome the changes in the leadership of the Soviet Union which eventually led to the end of the Cold War.

question_en: Who was the first Archbishop of Canterbury?
explanation_en: St Augustine was the first Archbishop of Canterbury.

question_en: Who was the first wife of Henry VIII?
explanation_en: Catherine of Aragon was the first wife of Henry VIII.

question_en: Which of the following flags is not represented in the union flag?
explanation_en: The Welsh dragon does not appear on the Union Flag because, when the first Union Flag was created in 1606 from the flags of Scotland and England, the Principality of Wales was already united with England.

question_en: When did ‘the Concorde’ aircraft fly for the first time?
explanation_en: The Concorde first flew in 1969 and began carrying passengers in 1976.

question_en: By what other name is the Conservative Party also known as?
explanation_en: The modern Conservative Party is still sometimes referred to as the Tories .

question_en: When did Adolf Hitler come to power in Germany?
explanation_en: Adolf Hitler came to power in Germany in 1933 .

question_en: What was inscribed in the first coins to be minted in Britain during the Iron Age?

options.en: The names of Iron Age Kings

explanation_en: They made the first coins to be minted in Britain, some inscribed with the names of Iron Age kings .

question_en: When did Emmeline Pankhurst set up the Women’s Franchise League, which fought to get the vote in local elections for married women?
explanation_en: Emmeline Pankhurst was born in Manchester in 1858. She set up the women‘s Franchise League in 1889 , which fought to get the vote in local elections for married women. In 1903 she helped found the Women‘s Social and Political Union (WSPU).

question_en: In which English county is the monument of Stonehenge located?
explanation_en: The ancestors of these first farmers probably came from south-east Europe. These people built houses, tombs and monuments on the land. One of these monuments,Stonehenge, still stands in what is now the English county of Wiltshire .

question_en: What is the best-known work from Robert Burns?
explanation_en: Burns‘ best-known work is probably the song Auld Lang Syne , which is sung by people in the UK and other countries when they are celebrating the New Year (or Hogmanay as it is called in Scotland).

question_en: Which of the following crosses does NOT form part of the Union Flag?

options.en: The cross of St George, patron saint of England
The cross of St David, patron saint of Wales
The cross of St Patrick, patron saint of Ireland
The cross of St Andrew, patron saint of Scotland

explanation_en: The cross of St David, patron saint of Wales does not form part of the Union Flag because, when the first Union Flag was created in 1606 from the flags of Scotland and England, the Principality of Wales was already untied with England.

question_en: What British actor was best known for his roles in various Shakespeare plays?
explanation_en: The Laurence Olivier Awards take place annually at different venues in London. There are a variety of categories, including best director, best actor and best actress. The awards are named after the British actor Sir Laurence Olivier , late Lord Olivier, who was best known for his roles in various Shakespeare plays.

question_en: What name is given to the period of rapid development of industry in Britain in the 18th and 19th centuries?
explanation_en: The Industrial Revolution was the rapid development of industry in Britain in the 18th and 19th centuries.

question_en: Who was responsible for the construction of the Great Western Railway?
explanation_en: Isambard Kingdom Brunel was responsible for constructing the Great Western Railway, which was the first major railway built in Britain.

question_en: Which TWO of the following forts form part of Hadrian’s Wall?
explanation_en: Parts of Hadrian‘s Wall include the forts of Housesteads and Vindolanda .

question_en: Why did Elizabeth I keep her cousin Mary prisoner for 20 years?

options.en: She did not agree with her political views
She suspected Mary of wanting to take over the English throne
She was a spy of the Spanish Armada

explanation_en: Elizabeth suspected Mary of wanting to take over the English throne , and kept her prisoner for 20 years.

question_en: What was the name of the first cloned mammal?
explanation_en: In 1996, two British scientists, Sir Ian Wilmot (1944-) and Keith Campbell (1954-2012), led a team which was the first to succeed in cloning a mammal, Dolly the sheep .

question_en: How many British casualties were recorded during the First World War?
explanation_en: During the First World War millions of people were killed or wounded, with more than 2 million British casualties .

question_en: When did the Vikings first visit Britain to raid coastal towns and take away goods and slaves?
explanation_en: The Vikings came from Denmark and Norway. They first visited Britain in AD 789 to raid coastal towns and take away goods and slaves.

question_en: Where can Admiral Nelson’s ship known as HMS Victory be visited?
explanation_en: Admiral Nelson’s ship, HMS Victory, can be visited in Portsmouth .

question_en: Who were the Puritans?

options.en: A group of local leaders that surged in Wales with strict religious views
A group of Protestants who advocated strict and simple religious doctrine and worship
A group of Catholics who advocated strict and simple religious doctrine and worship
A group of landowners from the 18th century

explanation_en: The Puritans were a group of Protestants who advocated strict and simple religious doctrine and worship . They did not agree with the king’s religious views and disliked his reforms of the Church of England.

question_en: What is the name of the battle where the Scottish led by Robert the Bruce defeated the English in 1314?
explanation_en: In 1314 the Scottish, led by Robert the Bruce, defeated the English at the Battle of Bannockburn .

question_en: Who took the British throne after the death of Henry VIII?
explanation_en: Henry VIII was succeeded by his son Edward VI .

question_en: Which of the following words comes from the Viking language?
explanation_en: Words such as Grimsby and Scunthorpe come from the Viking languages.

question_en: During the Crusades, European Christians fought for the control of:
explanation_en: During the Middle Ages many knights took part in the Crusades, in which European Christians fought for control of the Holy Land .

question_en: The line ‘to be or not to be’ belongs to which of the following plays or poems written by William Shakespeare?
explanation_en: To be or not to be’ is a line from William Shakespeare’s play Hamlet .

question_en: Charles I wanted the worship of the Church of England to include more ceremony and introduced a revised Prayer Book:
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: What was the name of the project through which the atomic bomb was developed?
explanation_en: Scientists led by Ernest Rutherford, working at Manchester and then Cambridge University, were the first to ‘split the atom’ and took part in the Manhattan Project in the United States, which developed the atomic bomb.

question_en: Who supported the Parliament during the Civil War that began in 1642?
explanation_en: A civil war between the king and Parliament began in 1642. The country split into those who supported the king (the Cavaliers) and those who supported Parliament (the Roundheads) .

question_en: What is the meaning of the term the ‘Divine Right of Kings’?

options.en: The idea that the king was directly appointed by God to rule
The idea that the king was appointed by landowners
The King could rule the country but with the support of the Parliament
The Parliament and the King shared responsibilities

explanation_en: The ‘Divine Right of Kings’ refers to the idea that the king was directly appointed by God to rule and that the king should be able to act without having to seek approval from Parliament.

question_en: Who was the first person to lead a Roman invasion in Britain in 55 BC?
explanation_en: Julius Caesar led a Roman invasion of Britain in 55 BC. This was unsuccessful and for nearly 100 years Britain remained separate from the Roman Empire.

question_en: When Henry VIII took the title ‘King of Ireland’. English laws were introduced and everyone except for local leaders were expected to follow the instructions of the Lord Lieutenants in Dublin:
explanation_en: False . When Henry VIII took the title ‘King of Ireland’. English laws were introduced and local leaders were expected to follow the instructions of the Lord Lieutenants in Dublin.

question_en: When was Lincoln Cathedral built?
explanation_en: Lincoln Cathedral was built in the middle ages or medieval times .

question_en: How old was Edward VI when he died?
explanation_en: Edward VI died at the age of 15 after ruling for just over six years, and his half-sister Mary became queen.

question_en: What name was given to those who supported the king during the Civil War that began in 1642?
explanation_en: During the Civil War that started in 1642 those who supported the king (the Cavaliers) and those who supported Parliament (the Roundheads).

question_en: By 1200, the English ruled an area of Scotland known as the Pale, around Edinburgh:
explanation_en: By 1200, the English ruled an area of Ireland known as the Pale, around Dublin .

question_en: After the Second World War, England was exhausted economically.
explanation_en: True . Although the UK had won the war, the country was exhausted economically after WWII.

question_en: Who was Florence Nightingale?

options.en: A scientist who proposed that enemy aircraft could be detected by radio waves
One of the tribal leaders who fought against the Romans
A nurse who worked in military hospitals, treating soldiers who were fighting in the Crimean War
A mathematician who was influential in the development of computer science

explanation_en: Florence Nightingale was a nurse who worked in military hospitals, treating soldiers who were fighting in the Crimean War .

question_en: Who was the Prime Minister of the UK from 1945 to 1951?
explanation_en: Clement Attlee was Prime Minister from 1945 to 1951 and led the Labour Party for 20 years.

question_en: Queen Elizabeth I was a Catholic:
explanation_en: False . Queen Elizabeth I was a Protestant.

question_en: When did King Edward I of England introduce the Statute of Rhuddlan by which Wales annexed to the Crown of England?
explanation_en: In 1284 King Edward I of England introduced the Statute of Rhuddlan, which annexed Wales to the Crown of England.

question_en: The settlements of Scottish and English Protestants in Ulster (the northern province of Ireland) during the reigns of Elizabeth I and James I, who took over the land from Catholic landholders is known as:
explanation_en: These settlements were known as plantations .

question_en: Why did Henry VIII decide to divorce Catherine of Aragon?

options.en: She was unpopular in the country
She was too old to give him another child
She was accused of taking lovers to the Palace

explanation_en: When Catherine was too old to give him another child , Henry decided to divorce her, hoping that another wife would give him a son to be his heir.

question_en: What characterised the Bronze Age?

options.en: People were farmers who lived in large villages
People lived in roundhouses and buried their dead in tombs

explanation_en: During the Bronze Age people lived in roundhouses and buried their dead in tombs called round barrows.

question_en: How many British citizens left the country between 1853 and 1913?
explanation_en: Between 1853 and 1913, as many as 13 million British citizens left the country.

question_en: Who was known as the Iron Duke?
explanation_en: In 1815, the French Wars ended with the defeat of the Emperor Napoleon by the Duke of Wellington at the Battle of Waterloo. Wellington was known as the Iron Duke and later became Prime Minister.

question_en: Which of the following is NOT a British invention?
explanation_en: The World Wide Web, the Turing machine and the hovercraft are British inventions. The mobile phone is not.

question_en: When did Argentina invade the Falklands Islands?
explanation_en: In 1982 , Argentina invaded the Falkland Islands, a British overseas territory in the South Atlantic.

question_en: When did Alexander Fleming win the Nobel Prize in medicine for the discovery of penicillin?
explanation_en: Fleming won the Nobel Prize in Medicine in 1945 for the discovery of the penicillin.

question_en: When did the Roman army leave Britain?
explanation_en: The Roman army left Britain in AD 410 to defend other parts of the Roman Empire and never returned.

question_en: When was Emmeline Pankhurst born?
explanation_en: Emmeline Pankhurst was born in Manchester in 1858 . She set up the women‘s Franchise League in 1889, which fought to get the vote in local elections for married women.

question_en: How long did the First World War last for?
explanation_en: The First World War lasted four years (1914 – 1918) .

question_en: Who was Henry VII?

options.en: The leader of the House of York
The leader of the House of Lancaster
The leader of the House of Buckingham
The leader of the House of Leeds

explanation_en: Henry VII was the leader of the House of Lancaster .

question_en: Which of the following areas did the British Empire NOT cover during the Victorian period?
explanation_en: During the Victorian period, the British Empire grew to cover all of India, Australia and large parts of Africa.

question_en: Which British scientist led the development of the atomic bomb?
explanation_en: Scientists led by Ernest Rutherford , working at Manchester and then Cambridge University, were the first to ‘split the atom‘ and took part in the Manhattan Project in the United States, which developed the atomic bomb.

question_en: After the Black Death, the growing wealth in the towns led to the development of a strong middle class:
explanation_en: True . After the Black Death, new social classes appeared, including owners of large areas of land (later called the gentry), and people left the countryside to live in the towns. In the towns, growing wealth led to the development of a strong middle class.

question_en: What was the ability of the Harrier jump jet aircraft?
explanation_en: The Harrier jump jet, an aircraft capable of taking off vertically , was also designed and developed in the UK.

question_en: Which TWO of Henry VIII’s wives were accused of taking lovers and executed?
explanation_en: Anne Boleyn and Catherine Howard were accused of taking lovers and executed.

question_en: Which two scientists led a team which was the first to succeed in cloning a mammal, Dolly the sheep?

options.en: Sir Frank Whittle and Sir Christopher Cockrell
Sir Robert Edwards and Patrick Steptoe
Sir Ian Wilmot and Keith Campbell
Francis Creek and John Logie Baird

explanation_en: In 1996, two British scientists, Sir Ian Wilmot (1944-) and Keith Campbell (1954-2012), led a team which was the first to succeed in cloning a mammal, Dolly the sheep.

question_en: What British discovery was influential in the development of computer science and the modern-day computer?
explanation_en: The Turing machine is a theoretical mathematical device invented by Alan Turing (1912-54), a British mathematician, in the 1930s. The theory was influential in the development of computer science and the modern-day computer.

question_en: Which famous philosopher developed ideas about human nature during the 18th century and that have continued to influence philosophers over the years?
explanation_en: During the 18th century, David Hume ‘s ideas about human nature continue to influence philosophers.

question_en: When did the ‘Concorde‘ aircraft carry passengers for the first time?
explanation_en: The ‘Concorde‘ began carrying passengers in 1976 .

question_en: Which prehistoric village located in northern Europe has helped archaeologists to understand more about how people lived near the end of the Stone Age?
explanation_en: Skara Brae on Orkney, off the north coast of Scotland, is the best preserved prehistoric village in northern Europe, and has helped archaeologists to understand more about how people lived near the end of the Stone Age.

question_en: During the Middle Ages, a Parliament was developed in Scotland, which had three Houses called Estates. These were:

options.en: The lords, the commons and the clergy
The lords, the commons and the farmers
The farmers, the blacksmiths and the clergy
The farmers, the blacksmiths and the teachers

explanation_en: The Parliament developed in Scotland in the Middle Ages had three Houses, called Estates: the lords, the commons and the clergy .

question_en: Which of the following scientific discoveries helped the progress of the Industrial Revolution?
explanation_en: Scientific discoveries, such as James Watt‘s work on steam power , helped the progress of the Industrial Revolution.

question_en: Where in England was one of the Anglo-Saxon kings buried with treasure and armour?
explanation_en: The burial place of one of the Anglo-Saxon kings was at Sutton Hoo in modern Suffolk .

question_en: Which of the following words comes from the Norman French (choose ONE option)?
explanation_en: Some words in modern English – for example, ‘park‘ and ‘beauty‘ – are based on Norman French words.

question_en: Catherine Parr, the sixth wife of Henry VIII, was Anne Boleyn’s cousin:
explanation_en: False . Catherine Howard was a cousin of Anne Boleyn.

question_en: What was the symbol of York during the Wars of the Roses?
explanation_en: The symbol of York during the Wars of the Roses was a white rose .

question_en: During which movement the Parliament passed laws giving women the right to equal pay and made it illegal for employees to discriminate against women because of their gender:
explanation_en: The Swinging Sixties was a time when the Parliament passed new laws giving women the right to equal pay and made it illegal for employers to discriminate against women because of their gender.

question_en: Where can you find a statue of Boudicca, the queen of the Iceni and one of the tribal leaders who fought against the Romans?
explanation_en: There is a statue of Boudicca, the queen of the Iceni, on Westminster Bridge in London, near the Houses of Parliament.

question_en: For how long was Britain a republic under the leadership of Oliver Cromwell?
explanation_en: Britain was a republic under the leadership of Oliver Cromwell for 11 years .

question_en: Who became one of the most popular monarchs in English history, particularly after 1588, when the English defeated the Spanish Armada?
explanation_en: Elizabeth I became one of the most popular monarchs in English history, particularly after 1588, when the English defeated the Spanish Armada (a large fleet of ships), which had been sent by Spain to conquer England and restore Catholicism.

question_en: When did the ‘Concorde‘ aircraft retire from service?
explanation_en: The ‘Concorde‘ was retired from service in 2003 .

question_en: When did the Allies defeat Germany marking the end of the Second World War?
explanation_en: Following victory on the beaches of Normandy, the allied forces pressed on through France and eventually into Germany. The Allies comprehensively defeated Germany in May 1945 .

question_en: During the Middle Ages, England was an important trading nation and people came to England from abroad to trade and also to work. Where did the weavers come from?
explanation_en: During the Middle Ages, people came to England from abroad to trade and also to work. Many had special skills, such as weavers from France , engineers from Germany, glass manufacturers from Italy and canal builders from Holland.

question_en: Throughout the 1990s, Britain played a leading role in coalition forces involved in the liberation of Kuwait, following the Iraqi invasion in 1990, and the conflict in the former Republic of Yugoslavia:
explanation_en: True . Throughout the 1990s, Britain played a leading role in coalition forces involved in the liberation of Kuwait, following the Iraqi invasion in 1990, and the conflict in the former Republic of Yugoslavia.

question_en: The ‘swinging sixties’ was a period of significant social change and it was known for its growth in:

options.en: British fashion, cinema and popular music

explanation_en: The decade of the 1960s was a period of significant social change. It was known as the ‘swinging sixties’. There was growth in British fashion, cinema and popular music .

question_en: Which prehistoric village located in northern Europe has helped archaeologists to understand more about how people lived near the end of the Stone Age?
explanation_en: Skara Brae on Orkney, off the north coast of Scotland, is the best preserved prehistoric village in northern Europe, and has helped archaeologists to understand more about how people lived near the end of the Stone Age.

question_en: During the Middle Ages, a Parliament was developed in Scotland, which had three Houses called Estates. These were:

options.en: The lords, the commons and the clergy
The lords, the commons and the farmers
The farmers, the blacksmiths and the clergy
The farmers, the blacksmiths and the teachers

explanation_en: The Parliament developed in Scotland in the Middle Ages had three Houses, called Estates: the lords, the commons and the clergy .

question_en: Which of the following scientific discoveries helped the progress of the Industrial Revolution?
explanation_en: Scientific discoveries, such as James Watt’s work on steam power , helped the progress of the Industrial Revolution.

question_en: Who where the parents of Elizabeth I?

options.en: Henry VII and Anne of Cleves
Henry VIII and Catherine of Aragon

explanation_en: Henry VIII and Anne Boleyn were the parents of Elizabeth I.

question_en: Which of the following areas did the British Empire NOT occupy during the Victorian period?
explanation_en: During the Victorian period, the British Empire grew to cover all of India, Australia and large parts of Africa.

question_en: Who were the first people to live in Britain in what we call the Stone Age?
explanation_en: The first people to live in Britain were hunter-gatherers , in what we call the Stone Age.

question_en: Which of the following statements regarding the Black Death is NOT true?

options.en: One third of the population of England died and a similar proportion in Scotland and Wales
It was one of the worst disasters ever to strike Britain
It affected children and old people only
Following the Black Death, there were labour shortages

explanation_en: In 1348, a disease, probably a form of plague, came to Britain. This was known as the Black Death. One third of the population of England died and a similar proportion in Scotland and Wales. This was one of the worst disasters ever to strike Britain. Following the Black Death, the smaller population meant there was less need to grow cereal crops. There were labour shortages and peasants began to demand higher wages.

question_en: Who was reigning in Britain when Wales became formally united with England by the Act for the Government of Wales?
explanation_en: During the reign of Henry VIII , Wales became formally united with England by the Act for the Government of Wales.

question_en: The Elizabethan period is known for the richness of its poetry and drama, especially for the plays and poems of which playwright?
explanation_en: The Elizabethan period is also remembered for the richness of its poetry and drama, especially the plays and poems of William Shakespeare .

question_en: Which flag has a diagonal red cross on a white ground?

options.en: The cross of St George, patron saint of England
The cross of St Patrick, patron saint of Ireland
The cross of St David, patron saint of Wales
The cross of St Andrew, patron saint of Scotland

explanation_en: The cross of St Patrick, patron saint of Ireland , is a diagonal red cross on a white ground.

question_en: What is the name of the best preserved prehistoric village in northern Europe and which origin traces back to the Stone Age?
explanation_en: Skara Brae on Orkney, off the north coast of Scotland, is the best preserved prehistoric village in northern Europe, and has helped archaeologists to understand more about how people lived near the end of the Stone Age.

question_en: After the Black Death, new social classes appeared in England, including owners of large areas of land known as:
explanation_en: Following the Black Death, the smaller population meant there was less need to grow cereal crops. There were labour shortages and peasants began to demand higher wages. New social classes appeared, including owners of large areas of land (later called the gentry ), and people left the countryside to live in the towns. In the towns, growing wealth led to the development of a strong middle class.

question_en: Elizabeth I was the younger daughter of which King?
explanation_en: Elizabeth I was the younger daughter of Henry VIII .

question_en: When did the First World War end?
explanation_en: The First World War ended at 11.00 am on 11th November 1918 with victory for Britain and its allies.

question_en: Which British scientist was awarded a Nobel Prize for discovering the structure of the DNA molecule?
explanation_en: The structure of the DNA molecule was discovered in 1953 through work at British universities in London and Cambridge. This discovery contributed to many scientific advances, particularly in medicine and fighting crime. Francis Crick (1916-2004), one of those awarded the Nobel Prize for this discovery, was British.

question_en: When did people learn how to make bronze?
explanation_en: Around 4,000 years ago , people learned to make bronze. We call this period the Bronze Age.

question_en: Who was Boudicca?

options.en: One of the tribal leaders who fought against the Romans
One of the architects who built Hadrian’s Wall
One of the tribal leaders who fought against the Vikings

explanation_en: One of the tribal leaders who fought against the Romans was Boudicca, the queen of the Iceni in what is now eastern England.

question_en: What actions did Henry VII take after his victory in the Wars of the Roses?

options.en: Reduced the power of the nobles
Broke away with the Church of Rome
Increased the power of the nobles

explanation_en: After his victory in the Wars of the Roses, Henry VII wanted to make sure that England remained peaceful and that his position as king was secure. He deliberately strengthened the central administration of England and reduced the power of the nobles .

question_en: Who became Prime Minister of the UK in May 2010?
explanation_en: In May 2010, and for the first time in the UK since February 1974, no political party won an overall majority in the General Election. The Conservative and Liberal Democrat parties formed a coalition and the leader of the Conservative Party, David Cameron , became Prime Minister.

question_en: How were Elizabeth I and ‘Mary, Queen of Scots’ related?
explanation_en: Mary, Queen of Scots’ was Elizabeth I’s cousin.

question_en: Choose the correct answer, the Middle Ages was:

options.en: A period of peace in Britain
A period where liberal ideas were introduced in Britain
A period where new ideas about politics and philosophy were introduced in Britain

explanation_en: The Middle Ages (or the medieval period) was a time of almost constant war .

question_en: What does the acronym NATO stand for?
explanation_en: NATO stands for The North Atlantic Treaty Organisation .

question_en: What characterised the people from the Bronze Age?

options.en: They were farmers who lived in large villages

explanation_en: The people of the Bronze Age were accomplished metalworkers who made many beautiful objects in bronze and gold, including tools, ornaments and weapons.

question_en: What is the name of the event that refers to the 6 June 1944 when Allied Forces landed in Normandy aiming to attack Hitler’s forces in Western Europe during World War II?
explanation_en: ‘D-Day’ refers to the 6th of June 1944, when allied forces landed in Normandy aiming to attack Hitler’s forces in Western Europe during World War II.

question_en: Which British Prime Minister led the Conservative government from 1979 to 1990?
explanation_en: Margaret Thatcher , Britain’s first woman Prime Minister, led the Conservative government from 1979 to 1990.

question_en: Which of the following civilisations is known for having built roads and public buildings, creating a structure of law and having introduced new plants and animals in Britain?
explanation_en: The Romans remained in Britain for 400 years. They built roads and public buildings, created a structure of law, and introduced new plants and animals.

question_en: What marked the beginning of what is called ‘constitutional monarchy’?

options.en: The laws passed after the Glorious Revolution
A speech given by the King
The emergence of new ideas about politics, philosophy and science
The development of the Bessemer process

explanation_en: The laws passed after the Glorious Revolution are the beginning of what is called ‘constitutional monarchy’.

question_en: When was the Turing machine discovered?
explanation_en: A Turing machine is a theoretical mathematical device invented by Alan Turing (1912-54), a British mathematician, in the 1930s .

question_en: During which period were the House of Lords and House of Commons established?
explanation_en: In the Middle Ages , the numbers attending Parliament increased and two separate parts, known as Houses were established. These were the House of the Commons on the House of the Lords.

question_en: How many years did Queen Victoria reign for?
explanation_en: In 1837, Queen Victoria became queen of the UK at the age of 18. She reigned until 1901, almost 64 years .

question_en: What is the name of Admiral Nelson’s ship?
explanation_en: Admiral Nelson’s ship was known as the HMS Victory .

question_en: What are the British scientists Sir Ian Wilmot and Keith Campbell famous for?

options.en: The cloning of the first mammal, Dolly the sheep
The invention of the MRI (magnetic resonance imaging) scanner
The invention of the World Wide Web

explanation_en: Sir Ian Wilmot and Keith Campbell led a team which was the first to succeed in cloning a mammal, Dolly the sheep .

question_en: Which of the following was one of the commanders in the defeat of the Spanish Armada and one of the founders of England’s naval tradition?
explanation_en: Sir Francis Drake was one of the commanders in the defeat of the Spanish Armada and one of the founders of England’s naval tradition.

question_en: Who was the inventor of the World Wide Web?
explanation_en: The inventor of the World Wide Web, Sir Tim Berners-Lee (1955-), is British. Information was successfully transferred via the web for the first time on 25 December 1990.

question_en: When did the Wars of the Roses start?
explanation_en: In 1455 , a civil war was begun to decide who should be king of England. It was fought between the supporters of two families: the House of Lancaster and the House of York. This war was called the Wars of the Roses, because the symbol  of Lancaster was a red rose and the symbol of York was a white rose.

question_en: When did the UK join the European Economic Community (EEC)?
explanation_en: At first the UK did not wish to join the EEC but it eventually did so in 1973 .

question_en: Who defeated the French at the battle of Agincourt in 1415?
explanation_en: One of the most famous battles of the Hundred Years War was the Battle of Agincourt in 1415, where King Henry V ’s vastly outnumbered English army defeated the French.

question_en: Which British Prime Minister nationalised major industries including coal and steel?
explanation_en: Clement Attlee ’s government undertook the nationalisation of major industries (like coal and steel), created the National Health Service and implemented many of Beveridge’s plans for a stronger welfare state. Attlee also introduced measures to improve the conditions of workers.

question_en: Who was the first Archbishop of Canterbury?
explanation_en: St Augustine became the first Archbishop of Canterbury.

question_en: Which British explorer mapped the coast of Australia?
explanation_en: Captain James Cook mapped the coast of Australia and a few colonies were established there.

question_en: What were the main occupations of the people during the Iron Age?
explanation_en: During the Iron Age most people were farmers, craft workers or warriors .

question_en: What was the name of Sir Francis Drake’s ship and which was one of the first to sail around the world?
explanation_en: Sir Francis Drake, one of the commanders in the defeat of the Spanish Armada, was one of the founders of England’s naval tradition. His ship, the Golden Hind , was one of the first to sail right around (‘circumnavigate’) the world.

question_en: Why did Henry VIII marry Anne of Cleves?
explanation_en: Henry married Anne of Cleves for political reasons but divorced her soon after.

question_en: How many American colonies declared their independence in 1776, stating that people had a right to establish their own governments?
explanation_en: In 1776, 13 American colonies declared their independence, stating that people had a right to establish their own governments.

question_en: Where was Catherine of Aragon (the first wife of Henry VIII) from?
explanation_en: Catherine of Aragon was a Spanish princess .

question_en: When did the post-war economic boom come to an end?
explanation_en: In the late 1970s , the post-war economic boom came to an end.

question_en: During her government, Margaret Thatcher made structural changes to the economy through the privatisation of nationalised industries and imposed legal controls on trade union powers:
explanation_en: True . Margaret Thatcher made structural changes to the economy through the privatisation of nationalised industries and imposed legal controls on trade union powers.

question_en: Who sat at the House of Commons during the Middle Ages?

options.en: Knights and wealthy people from towns and cities
Everyone could sit at the House of Commons

explanation_en: In the Middle Ages, Knights , who were usually smaller landowners, and wealthy people from towns and cities were elected to sit in the House of Commons.

question_en: Which of the following words come from the Anglo-Saxon language?
explanation_en: The words ‘Apple’ and ‘summer’ are based on Anglo-Saxon words.

question_en: How old was the Queen of Scotland, Mary Stuart, when she became queen?
explanation_en: The queen of Scotland, Mary Stuart (often now called ‘Mary, Queen of Scots’) was a Catholic. She was only a week old when her father died and she became queen.

question_en: Which British playwright had a great influence on the English language and invented many words that are still common today?
explanation_en: William Shakespeare had a great influence on the English language and invented many words that are still common today.

question_en: What proportion of the population died as a result of the Black Death in England?
explanation_en: As a result of the Black Death, one third of the population of England died and a similar proportion in Scotland and Wales.

question_en: Who wrote a famous series of poems in English about a group of people going to Canterbury on a pilgrimage and known as ‘The Canterbury Tales’?
explanation_en: In the years leading up to 1400, Geoffrey Chaucer wrote a series of poems in English about a group of people going to Canterbury on a pilgrimage. The people decided to tell each other stories on the journey, and the poems describe the travellers and some of the stories they told.

question_en: Which of the following is NOT a British invention of the 20th century?
explanation_en: Radioactivity is not a British invention.

question_en: Which British Prime Minister was famous for the speech ‘I have nothing to offer but blood, toil, tears and sweat’?
explanation_en: During the Second World War, Winston Churchill made many famous speeches including lines which you may still hear:  ‘I have nothing to offer but blood, toil, tears and sweat’. This was Churchill’s first speech to the House of Commons after he became Prime Minister, 1940.

question_en: Which of the following countries did NOT belong to the Allies during World War II?
explanation_en: Bulgaria was not part of the Allies during the Second World War.

question_en: Which famous architect designed St Paul’s Cathedral after it was destroyed by a fire in 1666?
explanation_en: In 1666, a great fire destroyed much of the city of London, including many churches and St Paul’s Cathedral. London was rebuilt with a new St Paul’s, which was designed by a famous architect, Sir Christopher Wren .

question_en: Who proposed that enemy aircrafts could be detected by radio waves and is responsible for the development of the radar?
explanation_en: Radar was developed by Scotsman Sir Robert Watson-Watt (1892-1973), who proposed that enemy aircraft could be detected by radio waves. The first successful radar test took place in 1935.

question_en: Who sat at the House of Lords during the Middle Ages?

options.en: Knights, who were usually smaller landowners
Wealthy people from towns and cities

explanation_en: During the Middle Ages, great landowners and bishops sat in the House of Lords.

question_en: Which of the following countries was NOT part of the Central Powers during the First World War?
explanation_en: During the First World War Serbia formed part of the Allied powers.

question_en: Who was the first English Prime Minister?
explanation_en: The first man to be called this was Sir Robert Walpole , who was Prime Minister from 1721 to 1742.

question_en: Who was Robert Burns?
explanation_en: Known in Scotland as ‘The Bard’, Robert Burns was a Scottish poet .

question_en: Who established the Church of England?
explanation_en: To divorce his first wife, Henry VIII needed the approval of the Pope. When the Pope refused, Henry established the Church of England. In this new church, the king, not the Pope, would have the power to appoint bishops and order how people should worship.

question_en: What name is given to the period in the 18th century when new ideas about politics, philosophy and science were developed?
explanation_en: During the 18th century, new ideas about politics, philosophy and science were developed. This is often called ‘the Enlightenment’ .

question_en: What is the meaning of ‘Magna Carta’?
explanation_en: The Magna Carta (which means the Great Charter ). The Magna Carta established the idea that even the king was subject to the law. It protected the rights of the nobility and restricted the king’s power to collect taxes or to make and change laws.

question_en: When did the Wars of the Roses end?
explanation_en: The Wars of the Roses ended with the Battle of Bosworth Field in 1485 .

question_en: The UK is not a full member of the European Union because it does not use the Euro currency:
explanation_en: False . The UK is a full member of the European Union but does not use the Euro currency.

question_en: Who was the British Minister after Margaret Thatcher?
explanation_en: John Major was Prime Minister after Mrs Thatcher, and helped establish the Northern Ireland peace process.

question_en: Who defeated Emperor Napoleon at the Battle of Waterloo in 1815?
explanation_en: In 1815, the French Wars ended with the defeat of the Emperor Napoleon by the Duke of Wellington at the Battle of Waterloo.

question_en: What was the symbol of the House of Tudor?

options.en: A red rose with a white rose inside it
A white rose with a red rose inside it

explanation_en: The symbol of the House of Tudor was a red rose with a white rose inside it as a sign that the Houses of York and Lancaster were now allies.

question_en: What animal is represented in the Welsh flag?
explanation_en: There is also an official Welsh flag, which shows a Welsh dragon . The Welsh dragon does not appear on the Union Flag because, when the first Union Flag was created in 1606 from the flags of Scotland and England, the Principality of Wales was already untied with England.

question_en: Which famous economist developed ideas about economics during the 18th century and which are still referred to today?
explanation_en: During the 18th century, new ideas about politics, philosophy and science were developed. This is often called ‘the Enlightenment’. Many of the great thinkers of the Enlightenment were Scottish. Adam Smith developed ideas about economics which are still referred to today.

question_en: Who invented the cash-dispensing ATM (automatic teller machine) or ‘cashpoint’?
explanation_en: In the 1960s, James Goodfellow (1937-) invented the cash-dispensing ATM (automatic teller machine) or ‘cashpoint’. The first of these was put into use by Barclays Bank in Enfield, north London in 1967.

question_en: What is the meaning of the term ‘Habeas corpus’?

options.en: You must present the person in court

explanation_en: Habeas corpus is Latin for ‘you must present the person in court’ . The Habeas Corpus Act guaranteed that no one could be held prisoner unlawfully. Every prisoner has a right to a court hearing.

question_en: Which German princess got married with Henry VIII?
explanation_en: Anne of Cleves was a German princess who married Henry VIII for political reasons.

question_en: What was the most spoken language during the Iron Age?
explanation_en: The language spoken during the Iron Age was part of the Celtic language family.

question_en: Which of the following was one of the most famous battles of the Hundred Years War?
explanation_en: One of the most famous battles of the Hundred Years War was the Battle of Agincourt in 1415, where King Henry V’s vastly outnumbered English army defeated the French. The English left France in the 1450s.

question_en: During the Middle Ages, England was an important trading nation and people came to England from abroad to trade and also to work. Where did the engineers come from?
explanation_en: During the Middle Ages, people came to England from abroad to trade and also to work. Many had special skills, such as weavers from France, engineers from Germany , glass manufacturers from Italy and canal builders from Holland.

question_en: How many wives did Henry VIII have?
explanation_en: Henry VIII had six wives .

question_en: When did the English become the preferred language of the royal court and Parliament?
explanation_en: By 1400 , in England, official documents were being written in English, and English had become the preferred language of the royal court and Parliament.

question_en: What was Boudicca, the queen of the Iceni in what is now eastern England, known for?
explanation_en: One of the tribal leaders who fought against the Romans was Boudicca, the queen of the Iceni in what is now eastern England.

question_en: When was the hovercraft invented?
explanation_en: Sir Christopher Cockerell (1910-99), a British inventor, invented the hovercraft in the 1950s .

question_en: Who is the author of the famous play ‘Macbeth’?
explanation_en: Macbeth is a famous play by William Shakespeare .

question_en: What animal was cloned by scientists Sir Ian Wilmot and Keith Campbell?
explanation_en: In 1996, two British scientists, Sir Ian Wilmot (1944-) and Keith Campbell (1954-2012), led a team which was the first to succeed in cloning a mammal, Dolly the sheep .

question_en: What did the Chartists campaign for?

options.en: The right to vote for women over 30 years old
The right to vote for the working classes and other people without property
The right to vote for women and men over 30 years old
The right to vote for every citizen

explanation_en: The Chartists, demanded the vote for the working classes and other people without property .

question_en: When did Emmeline Pankhurst die?
explanation_en: Emmeline Pankhurst died in 1928 . Shortly before Emmeline’s death, women were given the right to vote at the age of 21, the same as men.

question_en: What TWO houses were confronted during the Wars of the Roses?
explanation_en: The Wars of the Roses were fought between the supporters of two families: the House of Lancaster and the House of York .

question_en: Who was the first woman to become Prime Minister in the UK?
explanation_en: Margaret Thatcher , Britain’s first woman Prime Minister, led the Conservative government from 1979 to 1990.

question_en: How old was Queen Victoria when she became Queen in 1837?
explanation_en: In 1837, Queen Victoria became queen of the UK at the age of 18 .

question_en: When did the First World War start?
explanation_en: On 28 June 1914 , Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria was assassinated. This set off a chain of events leading to the First World War (1914-18).

question_en: Which battle signified the end of the Wars of the Roses?
explanation_en: The Wars of the Roses ended with the Battle of Bosworth Field in 1485.

question_en: Who became Prime Minister in the UK in May 1940?
explanation_en: Winston Churchill was the son of a politician and, before becoming a Conservative MP in 1900, was a soldier and journalist. In May 1940 he became Prime Minister.

question_en: When did English settlers begin to colonise the eastern coast of America?
explanation_en: In Elizabeth I’s time , English settlers first began to colonise the eastern coast of America.

question_en: When did the Black Death come to England?
explanation_en: In 1348 , a disease, probably a form of plague, came to Britain. This was known as the Black Death.

question_en: When did British combat troops leave Iraq?
explanation_en: British combat troops left Iraq in 2009 .

question_en: The line ‘all the world’s a stage’ belongs to which of the following plays or poems written by William Shakespeare?
explanation_en: All the world’s a stage’ are lines from William Shakespeare’s play As You Like It .

question_en: During the times of Elizabeth I, English settlers began to colonise:
explanation_en: In Elizabeth I’s time, English settlers first began to colonise the eastern coast of America .

question_en: Which TWO families started a civil war to decide who should be king of England in 1455?
explanation_en: In 1455, a civil war was begun to decide who should be king of England. It was fought between the supporters of two families: the House of Lancaster and the House of York .

question_en: Who was in charge of the British fleet at the Battle of Trafalgar?
explanation_en: Admiral Nelson was in charge of the British fleet at Trafalgar and was killed in the battle.

question_en: Who was the leader of the English Republic?
explanation_en: Oliver Cromwell was the leader of the English republic.

question_en: Where was Margaret Thatcher born?
explanation_en: Margaret Thatcher was the daughter of a grocer from Grantham in Lincolnshire .

question_en: Who introduced the Statute of Rhuddlan by which Wales annexed to the Crown of England in 1284?
explanation_en: In 1284 King Edward I of England introduced the Statute of Rhuddlan, which annexed Wales to the Crown of England.

question_en: The decade of the 1960s was a period of significant social change, when there was growth in British fashion, cinema and popular music:
explanation_en: True . The decade of the 1960s was a period of significant social change. It was known as the ‘swinging sixties’. There was growth in British fashion, cinema and popular music.

question_en: When did Britain and France develop the world’s only supersonic commercial airliner known as ‘The Concorde’?
explanation_en: The 1960s was also a time of technological progress. Britain and France developed the world’s only supersonic commercial airliner, Concorde.

question_en: Which flag has a diagonal white cross on a blue ground?

options.en: The cross of St Andrew, patron saint of Scotland
The cross of St Patrick, patron saint of Ireland
The cross of St George, patron saint of England
The cross of St David, patron saint of Wales

explanation_en: The cross of St Andrew, patron saint of Scotland , is a diagonal white cross on a blue ground.

question_en: Which was the first major railway built in Britain?
explanation_en: The Great Western Railway , which was the first major railway built in Britain.

question_en: In the 15th century, Henry Tudor, the leader of the House of Lancaster, became:
explanation_en: In 1485, Henry Tudor, the leader of the House of Lancaster, became King Henry VII after defeating King Richard III of the House of York at the Battle of Bosworth Field.

question_en: Who defeated the Vikings?
explanation_en: King Alfred the Great defeated the Vikings.

question_en: During the Middle Ages, England was an important trading nation and people came to England from abroad to trade and also to work. Where did the canal builders come from?
explanation_en: During this Middle Ages, England was an important trading nation and people came to England from abroad to trade and also to work. Canal builders came from Holland .

question_en: The Elizabethan period in England was a time of growing patriotism: a feeling of pride in being English, English explorers sought new trade routes and tried to expand British trade into the Spanish colonies in the Americas:
explanation_en: This statement is TRUE .

question_en: How many crosses form the Union Flag?
explanation_en: The Union Flag has three crosses .

question_en: Who were the suffragettes?

options.en: A group who demanded the vote for the working classes
A group who used civil disobedience to gain the vote for women
A group who campaigned for the vote of men over 21 years of age
A group who introduced new ideas about politics

explanation_en: The suffragettes were a group who used civil disobedience to gain the vote for women .

question_en: When did Henry VIII die?
explanation_en: Henry VIII died on the 28th of January 1547 .

question_en: Which of the following lines from Shakespeare’s plays and poems is often still quoted?

options.en: I have nothing to offer but blood, toil, tears and sweat

explanation_en: The darling buds of May ’ is a line from William Shakespeare’s play Sonnet 18 – Shall I Compare Thee To A Summer’s Day.

question_en: Which of the following statements about Mary, Queen of the Scotland, is NOT true?

options.en: She spent most of her childhood in France
She gave her throne to her Protestant son, James VI of Scotland.
She was kept her prisoner for 20 years

explanation_en: The queen of Scotland, Mary Stuart (often now called ‘Mary, Queen of Scots’) was a Catholic .

question_en: When did Ireland become unified with England, Wales and Scotland?
explanation_en: In 1801 , Ireland became unified with England, Scotland and Wales after the Act of Union of 1800. This created the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland.

question_en: What was the estimated population of the British Empire during the Victorian period?
explanation_en: ﻿During the Victorian period, the British Empire became the largest empire the world has ever seen, with an estimated population of more than 400 million people .

question_en: Which of the following countries was NOT part of the Allied Powers during the First World War?
explanation_en: Bulgaria was part of the Central Powers.

question_en: When did the First World War finish?
explanation_en: The First World War ended at 11.00 am on 11th November 1918 with victory for Britain and its allies.

question_en: Florence Nightingale is known for her work on:

options.en: The development of nursing in the UK
The development and manufacture of motor-sport technology

explanation_en: Florence Nightingale is often regarded as the founder of modern nursing .

question_en: Which Act abolished slavery throughout the British Empire in 1833?
explanation_en: In 1833 the Emancipation Act abolished slavery throughout the British Empire.

question_en: Who became King of England when Queen Anne died in 1714?
explanation_en: When Queen Anne died in 1714, Parliament chose a German, George I , to be the next king, because he was Anne’s nearest Protestant relative.

question_en: Who discovered the penicillin in 1928?
explanation_en: Alexander Fleming was researching influenza (the ‘flu’) in 1928 when he discovered penicillin.

question_en: How many British casualties were recorded during the first day of the British attack of the Somme in July 1916 during the First World War?
explanation_en: During the First World War, one battle, the British attack of the Somme in July 1916, resulted in about 60,000 British casualties on the first day alone.

question_en: The Welsh dragon on the Welsh flag does not appear on the Union Flag because, when the first Union Flag was created in 1606 from the flags of Scotland and England, the Principality of Wales was already untied with England:
explanation_en: This statement is True .

question_en: Which two countries developed the ‘Concorde’ aircraft?
explanation_en: Britain and France developed the world’s only supersonic commercial airliner, Concorde.

question_en: Which Scottish physician and researcher co-discovered the insulin used to treat diabetes?
explanation_en: John Macleod (1876-1935) was the co-discoverer of insulin, used to treat diabetes.

question_en: Who was voted the greatest Briton of all time in 2002?
explanation_en: Winston Churchill was voted the greatest Briton of all time by the public in 2002.

question_en: Which of the following social laws were liberalised during the 1960s?
explanation_en: The 1960s was a time when social laws were liberalised, for example in relation to divorce and to abortion in England, Wales and Scotland.

question_en: Who was the mother of Elizabeth I?
explanation_en: Anne Boleyn and Henry VIII had one daughter, Elizabeth I.

question_en: When was the National Health System (NHS) established?
explanation_en: In 1948 , Aneurin (Nye) Bevan, the Minister for Health, led the establishment of the National Health Service (NHS), which guaranteed a minimum standard of health care for all, free at the point of use.

question_en: How many Russian and Polish Jews came to Britain to escape prosecution between 1870 and 1914?
explanation_en: Between 1870 and 1914, around 120,000 Russian and Polish Jews came to Britain to escape persecution.

question_en: The line ‘a rose by any other name’ belongs to which of the following plays or poems written by William Shakespeare?
explanation_en: A rose by any other name’ is a line from William Shakespeare’s play Romeo and Juliet .

question_en: Which British scientist made important discoveries working with steam power?
explanation_en: Scientific discoveries, such as James Watt ’s work on steam power, helped the progress of the Industrial Revolution.

question_en: When was the first television broadcast made?
explanation_en: In 1932 Scotsman John Logie Baird made the first television broadcast between London and Glasgow.

question_en: Who invented the hovercraft?
explanation_en: ﻿Sir Christopher Cockerell invented the hovercraft.

question_en: Which of the following events relates to the German bombing of London and other cities at night time during the World War II?
explanation_en: The Blitz﻿ relates to the German air force bombing London and other British cities at night-time during ‘the Battle of Britain’, in the summer of 1940.

question_en: Which of the following social changes took place during the ‘Swinging Sixties’?

options.en: The position of women in the workplace was improved
Everyone could study in the University for free
People from the same gender were allowed to get married

explanation_en: ﻿During the ‘Swinging Sixties’ the position of women in the workplace improved .

question_en: Where was Emmeline Pankhurst born?
explanation_en: Emmeline Pankhurst was born in Manchester in 1858.

question_en: Which of the following is a British invention from the 20th century?
explanation_en: The Harrier jump jet , an aircraft capable of taking off vertically, is a British invention of the 20th century.

question_en: When did Winston Churchill die?
explanation_en: Winston Churchill died in 1965 .

question_en: During the 17th century there were two main groups in Parliament, which were known as:

options.en: The Liberal Democrats and the Labour Party
The lefties and the right wing
The Conservative Party and the Liberal Democrats

explanation_en: During the 17th century there were two main groups in Parliament, known as the Whigs and the Tories .

question_en: When was the Women’s Social and Political Union (WSPU) established?
explanation_en: ﻿In 1903 Emmeline Pankhurst helped found the Women’s Social and Political Union (WSPU).

question_en: Under which Act was the United Kingdom of Great Britain created?
explanation_en: ﻿The Act of Union , known as the Treaty of Union in Scotland, was agreed in 1707, creating the Kingdom of Great Britain.

question_en: When did the first farmers arrive in Britain?
explanation_en: The first farmers arrived in Britain 6,000 years ago .

question_en: For which purpose was the Statute of Rhuddlan introduced?

options.en: To annex Scotland to the Crown of England
To annex Northern Ireland to the Crown of England
To annex Ireland to the Crown of England
To annex Wales to the Crown of England

explanation_en: In 1284 King Edward I of England introduced the Statute of Rhuddlan, which annexed Wales to the Crown of England .

question_en: When did Henry VIII start reining?
explanation_en: Henry VIII was king of England from 21 April 1509 until his death on 28 January 1547.

question_en: In May 2010, the Conservative and Liberal Democrat parties formed a coalition and the leader of the Liberal Democrat Party, David Cameron, became Prime Minister:
explanation_en: False . In May 2010, and for the first time in the UK since February 1974, no political party won an overall majority in the General Election. The Conservative and Liberal Democrat parties formed a coalition and the leader of the Conservative Party, David Cameron, became Prime Minister.

question_en: Which hill fort from the Iron Age can be seen in the county of Dorset?
explanation_en: A very impressive hill fort from the Iron Age can still be seen today at Maiden Castle , in the English county of Dorset.

question_en: What was the biggest source of employment in Britain before the 18th century?
explanation_en: Before the 18th century, agriculture was the biggest source of employment in Britain.

question_en: Why was the ‘Habeas Corpus Act’ of 1679 an important piece of legislation?

options.en: It allowed people over 21 years old to vote
It guaranteed that no one could be held prisoner unlawfully
It abolished slavery throughout the British Empire
It abolished a number of taxes on imported goods in the UK

explanation_en: The Habeas Corpus Act became law in 1679. This was a very important piece of legislation which remains relevant today. The Act guaranteed that no one could be held prisoner unlawfully . Every prisoner has a right to a court hearing.

question_en: Who was Prime Minister in England from 1721 to 1742?
explanation_en: The first man to be called this was Sir Robert Walpole , who was Prime Minister from 1721 to 1742.

question_en: The Battle of Hastings  is commemorated in a great piece of embroidery, which is known as:
explanation_en: The Battle of Hastings is commemorated in a great piece of embroidery, known as the Bayeux tapestry , which can still be seen in France today.

question_en: Which of the following countries did NOT join the European Economic Community when it was first formed in 1957 (choose ONE option)?
explanation_en: West Germany, France, Belgium, Italy, Luxembourg and the Netherlands formed the European Economic Community (EEC) in 1957. At first the UK did not wish to join the EEC but it eventually did so in 1973.

question_en: When was Ireland divided into two countries?
explanation_en: In 1921 a peace treaty was signed and in 1922 Ireland became two countries.

question_en: The Middle Ages or medieval times relate to the time that goes from the period after the Norman Conquest until the year:
explanation_en: The period after the Norman Conquest up until about 1485 is called the Middle Ages (or the medieval period).

question_en: The action of handing out leaflets in the street or knocking on people’s doors to ask for their political support is known as:
explanation_en: The action of handing out leaflets in the street or knocking on people’s doors to ask for their political support is known as canvassing .

question_en: Who defeated the Vikings?
explanation_en: King Alfred the Great defeated the Vikings.

question_en: When was Queen Elizabeth II’s Diamond Jubilee celebrated?
explanation_en: Queen Elizabeth II’s Diamond Jubilee was celebrated in 2012 .

question_en: During the Middle Ages, England was an important trading nation and people came to England from abroad to trade and also to work. Where did the canal builders come from?
explanation_en: During the Middle Ages, England was an important trading nation and people came to England from abroad to trade and also to work. Canal builders came from Holland .

question_en: The Elizabethan period in England was a time of growing patriotism: a feeling of pride in being English, English explorers sought new trade routes and tried to expand British trade into the Spanish colonies in the Americas:
explanation_en: This statement is TRUE .

question_en: Which of the following are ‘Crown dependencies’?

options.en: The Isle of Man and the Channel Islands
The Isle of Wight and Northern Ireland

explanation_en: The Isle of Man and the Channel Islands are ‘Crown dependencies’.

question_en: Which of the following is a charity that works with old people?
explanation_en: Age UK is a charity that works with old people.

question_en: Which popular British sport can be traced back to the 15th century?
explanation_en: The modern game of golf can be traced back to 15th century Scotland.

question_en: How many crosses form the Union Flag?
explanation_en: The Union Flag has three crosses .

question_en: Who were the suffragettes?

options.en: A group who demanded the vote for the working classes
A group who used civil disobedience to gain the vote for women
A group who campaigned for the vote of men over 21 years of age
A group who introduced new ideas about politics

explanation_en: The suffragettes were a group who used civil disobedience to gain the vote for women .

question_en: What does the UK offer to its permanent residents or citizens?

options.en: A right to a fair trial

explanation_en: A right to a fair trial is the correct answer.

question_en: Which of the following meals is made of a fried meal with bacon, eggs, sausage,black pudding, tomatoes, mushrooms, soda bread and potato bread?
explanation_en: Ulster fry is a fried meal from Northern Ireland with bacon, eggs, sausage, black pudding, tomatoes, mushrooms, soda bread and potato bread.

question_en: What can living people donate that may help other people with injuries or illnesses (choose TWO options)?
explanation_en: Living people can donate blood and a kidney .

question_en: Where is the office of the MPs located?
explanation_en: The MP’s office is located at The House of Commons, Westminster , London, SW1A OAA.

question_en: Which TWO of the following statements is TRUE with regards to Magistrates and Justices of the Peace (JPs) in England, Wales and Scotland?

options.en: They do not need legal qualifications

explanation_en: In England, Wales and Scotland Magistrates and Justices of the Peace (JPs) usually work unpaid and do not need legal qualifications.

question_en: Which of the following is a showcase of mainly theatre and comedy performances?
explanation_en: The Fringe is a showcase of mainly theatre and comedy performances.

question_en: Which of the following days are public holidays in Scotland (choose TWO options)?
explanation_en: In Scotland, the 1st of January and the 2nd of January are bank holidays.

question_en: The House of Commons is normally more independent of the government than the House of Lords:
explanation_en: The House of Lords is normally more independent of the government than the House of Commons.

question_en: When was the Turing machine invented?
explanation_en: A Turing machine is a theoretical mathematical device invented by Alan Turing (1912-54), a British mathematician, in the 1930s .

question_en: During which period were the House of Lords and House of Commons established?
explanation_en: In the Middle Ages , the numbers attending Parliament increased and two separate parts, known as Houses were established. These were the House of the Commons and the House of the Lords.

question_en: What name is given to the period of time when Christians take time to reflect and prepare for Easter?
explanation_en: Lent is a time when Christians take time to reflect and prepare for Easter.

question_en: In the new Church of England created by Henry VIII, who had the power to appoint bishops and order how people should worship?
explanation_en: In the new Church of England, the king , not the Pope, would have the power to appoint bishops and order how people should worship.

question_en: In Wales, many people speak Gaelic – a completely different language from English – and it is taught in schools and universities:
explanation_en: In Wales, many people speak Welsh – a completely different language from English – and it is taught in schools and universities.

question_en: How many years did Queen Victoria reign for?
explanation_en: In 1837, Queen Victoria became queen of the UK at the age of 18. She reigned until 1901, almost 64 years .

question_en: The UK population is ethnically diverse and changing rapidly, especially in large cities such as London:
explanation_en: The UK population is ethnically diverse and changing rapidly, especially in large cities such as London.

question_en: Which of the following is NOT a role of the King:

options.en: To inaugurate important business in the UK
To receive foreign ambassadors and high commissioners
To entertain visiting heads of state

explanation_en: The King receives foreign ambassadors and high commissioners, entertains visiting heads of state, and makes state visits overseas in support of diplomatic and relationships with other counties.

question_en: The Speaker is a neutral MP and does not represent a political party:
explanation_en: The Speaker is neutral and does not represent a political party, even though he or she is an MP, represents a constituency and deals with the constituents’ problems like any other MP.

question_en: What is the height of the London Eye?
explanation_en: The London Eye is situated on the southern bank of the River Thames and is a Ferris wheel that is 43 feet (135 metres) tall.

question_en: How can a person facing domestic violence find a safe place to stay (choose TWO options)?

options.en: Going to a hotel away from their home
Contacting the National Domestic Violence Freephone Helpline

explanation_en: If you are facing domestic violence you can get help from the 24-hour National Domestic Violence Freephone Helpline on 0808 2000 247 at any time, or the police can help you find a safe place to stay.

question_en: What name is given to the celebration when people in Great Britain set off fireworks at home or in special displays on the 5th of November?
explanation_en: Bonfire Night , 5 November, is an occasion when people in Great Britain set off fireworks at home or in special displays.

question_en: When did the Vikings first visit Britain to raid coastal towns and take away goods and slaves?
explanation_en: The Vikings came from Denmark and Norway. They first visited Britain in AD 789 to raid coastal towns and take away goods and slaves.

question_en: Who has control over policy and laws governing defence, foreign affairs, immigration, taxation and social security in Northern Ireland?
explanation_en: Policy and laws governing defence, foreign affairs, immigration, taxation and social security all remain under central UK government control . However, many other public services, such as education, are controlled by the devolved administrations.

question_en: How many National Parks are there in England, Wales and Scotland?
explanation_en: There are 15 national parks in England, Wales and Scotland. They are areas of protected countryside that everyone can visit, and where people live, work and look after the landscape.

question_en: Which services can be obtained from veterinary surgeons?

options.en: They are places where pets can be left when you go on holidays
They are places where vaccinations and medical treatment for animals is available
They are places specifically prepare to groom pets

explanation_en: Vaccinations and medical treatment for animals are available from veterinary surgeons (vets).

question_en: The devolved governments in Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland cannot control policies and laws governing taxation and social security:
explanation_en: Policy and laws governing defence, foreign affairs, immigration, taxation and social security all remain under central UK government control. However, many other public services, such as education, are controlled by the devolved administrations.

question_en: The longer life expectancy in the UK has an effect on (choose TWO options):
explanation_en: People in the UK are living longer than ever before. This is due to improved living standards and better health care. There are now a record number of people aged 85 and over. This has an impact on the cost of pensions and health care .

question_en: What percentage of the total British population is located in Scotland?
