explanation_en: The population is very unequally distributed over the four parts of the UK. England more or less consistently makes up 84% of the total population, Wales around 5%, Scotland just over 8% , and Northern Ireland less than 3%.

question_en: When Cromwell died, his son, Richard, became Lord Protector in his place but was not able to control the army or the government:
explanation_en: This is true .

question_en: Which of the following academy awards are the equivalent of the Oscars in the UK?
explanation_en: The annual British Academy Film Awards, hosted by the British Academy of Film and Television Arts (BAFTA) , are the British equivalent of the Oscars.

question_en: Which political party was elected after the Second World War?
explanation_en: The Labour Party won the 1945 election. Clement Attlee was Prime Minister from 1945 to 1951 and led the Labour Party for 20 years.

question_en: Which British sportsman was captain of the English cricket team?
explanation_en: Sir Ian Botham captained the English cricket team and holds number of English Test cricket records, both for batting and for bowling.

question_en: In which Japanese cities did the United States drop atomic bombs in August 1945?
explanation_en: The war against Japan ended in August 1945 when the United States dropped its newly developed atom bombs on the Japanese cities of Hiroshima and Nagasaki .

question_en: What is a bank holiday (choose TWO answers)?

options.en: A day when banks are closed

explanation_en: In the UK, there are public holidays each year called bank holidays, when banks and many other businesses are closed for the day.

question_en: By 1200, the English ruled an area of Scotland known as the Pale, around Edinburgh:
explanation_en: By 1200, the English ruled an area of Ireland known as the Pale, around Dublin .

question_en: Which of the following countries does not take part in the Six Nations Championship?
explanation_en: The most famous rugby union competition is the Six Nations Championship between England, Ireland, Scotland, Wales, France and Italy.

question_en: Who became Queen after Edward VI?
explanation_en: Edward VI died at the age of 15 after ruling for just over six years, and his half-sister Mary became queen .

question_en: Which of the following is NOT classified as a criminal offence in the UK?

options.en: Selling alcohol to anyone under the age of 18
Unfair dismissal or discrimination in the workplace

explanation_en: Cases of unfair dismissal or discrimination in the workplace are considered civil offences .

question_en: The right to life and the right to liberty and security are some of the principles included in the European Convention of Human Rights:
explanation_en: This is true .

question_en: British values and principles are based on history and traditions and are protected by law, customs and expectations.
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: When did the first Christian communities begin to appear in Britain?
explanation_en: It was during the 3rd and 4th centuries AD that the first Christian communities began to appear in Britain.

question_en: Which of the following is NOT a devolved administration?
explanation_en: There are three devolved administrations in the UK, this include: the Welsh government, the Scottish Parliament and the Northern Ireland Assembly

question_en: Who built a wall in the north of England to keep out the Picts (ancestors of the Scottish people)?
explanation_en: Areas of what is now Scotland were never conquered by the Romans, and the Emperor Hadrian built a wall in the north of England to keep out the Picts (ancestors of the Scottish people).

question_en: Who looks after Edinburgh Castle?
explanation_en: Edinburgh castle is a dominant feature of the skyline in Edinburgh, Scotland. It has a long history, dating back to the early Middle Ages. It is looked after by Historic Environment Scotland .

question_en: Each MP represents a parliamentary constituency, which is a small area of the country:
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: Who were the pioneers of the IVF (in-vitro fertilisation) therapy?

options.en: Sir Frank Whittle and Sir Christopher Cockerell
Sir Robert Edwards and Patrick Steptoe
Francis Creek and John Logie Baird
Sir Ian Wilmot and Keith Campbell

explanation_en: IVF (in-vitro fertilisation) therapy for the treatment of infertility was pioneered in Britain by physiologist Sir Robert Edwards (1925-) and gynaecologist Patrick Steptoe (1913-88). The world’s first ‘test-tube baby’ was born in Oldham, Lancashire in 1978.

question_en: Who led the establishment of the National Health System (NHS) in 1948?
explanation_en: In 1948, Aneurin (Nye) Bevan , the Minister for Health, led the establishment of the National Health Service (NHS), which guaranteed a minimum standard of health care for all, free at the point of use.

question_en: Which of the following countries were granted their independence in 1947 (choose THREE answers)?
explanation_en: In 1947, independence was granted to nine countries, including India, Pakistan and Ceylon (now Sri Lanka) .

question_en: In the UK, bank holidays of no religious significance are celebrated at the beginning of May, in late May or early June, and in August:
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: Where can Admiral Nelson’s ship known as HMS Victory be visited?
explanation_en: Admiral Nelson’s ship, HMS Victory, can be visited in Portsmouth .

question_en: Who were the Puritans?

options.en: A group of Catholics who advocated strict and simple religious doctrine and worship
A group of Protestants who advocated strict and simple religious doctrine and worship
A group of landowners from the 18th century
A group of local leaders that surged in Wales with strict religious views

explanation_en: The Puritans were a group of Protestants who advocated strict and simple religious doctrine and worship . They did not agree with the king’s religious views and disliked his reforms of the Church of England.

question_en: What TWO different types of rugby are practised in the UK?
explanation_en: There are two different types of rugby, which have different rules: union and league .

question_en: What do William Blake, John Keats and Lord Byron have in common?

options.en: They were British poets of the 19th century
They were British painters of the Swinging Sixties
They were musicians of the middle ages

explanation_en: Poetry was very popular in the 19th century, with poets such as William Blake, John Keats, Lord Byron, Percy Shelley, Alfred Lord Tennyson, and Robert and Elizabeth Browning.

question_en: The Laurence Olivier Awards take place annually at different venues in Liverpool:
explanation_en: The Laurence Olivier Awards take place annually at different venues in London .

question_en: An additional rented piece of land where people can grow fruit and vegetables is known as:
explanation_en: In the UK, some people rent additional land called ‘an allotment’ , where they grow fruit and vegetables.

question_en: What is the name of the battle where the Scottish led by Robert the Bruce defeated the English in 1314?
explanation_en: In 1314 the Scottish, led by Robert the Bruce, defeated the English at the Battle of Bannockburn .

question_en: Who took the English throne after the death of Henry VIII?
explanation_en: Henry VIII was succeeded by his son Edward VI .

question_en: Carrying a weapon is a criminal offence, unless it is for self-defence:
explanation_en: It is a criminal offence to carry a weapon of any kind, even if it is for self-defence. This includes a gun, a knife or anything that is made or adapted to cause injury.

question_en: Prohibition of thought, conscience and religion is one of the principles included in the European Convention of Human Rights:
explanation_en: Freedom of thought , conscience and religion is one of the principles included in the European Convention of Human Rights.

question_en: Which TWO pop music groups became popular in the 1960s?
explanation_en: Two well-known pop music groups in the 1960s were The Beatles and The Rolling Stones .

question_en: Which of the following words comes from the Viking language?
explanation_en: Words such as Grimsby and Scunthorpe come from the Viking languages.

question_en: Eid ul Adha is the day when Muslims thank Allah for giving them the strength to complete the fast:
explanation_en: The day when Muslims thank Allah for giving them the strength to complete the fast is called Eid a-Fitr .

question_en: Will a non-UK national living in the UK and looking for work, starting work or setting up as self-employed need a National Insurance number?
explanation_en: A non-UK national living in the UK and looking for work, starting work or setting up as self-employed will need a National Insurance number. However, you can start work without one.

question_en: What is the name of the highest mountain in Wales?
explanation_en: Snowdon is the highest mountain in Wales.

question_en: Where did the Vikings come from?
explanation_en: The Vikings came from Denmark and Norway .

question_en: Who led a Scottish army against Oliver Cromwell just after England had been declared a Republic?
explanation_en: Charles II was crowned king of Scotland and led a Scottish army into England. Cromwell defeated this army in the Battles of Dunbar and Worcester.

question_en: How many pence are there in a pound?
explanation_en: There are 100 pence in a pound.

question_en: When are local elections for councillors held in most local authorities?
explanation_en: For most local authorities, local elections for councillors are held in May every year.

question_en: What sort of stories were collected by the Canterbury Tales written by Geoffrey Chaucer?
explanation_en: In the years leading up to 1400, Geoffrey Chaucer wrote a series of poems in English about a group of people going to Canterbury on a pilgrimage. The people decided to tell each other stories on the journey, and the poems describe the travellers and some of the stories they told . This collection poems is called The Canterbury Tales.

question_en: When did rugby originate?
explanation_en: Rugby originated in England in the early 19th century and is very popular in the UK today.

question_en: Which of the following lines does NOT form part of the citizenship pledge?

options.en: I will give my loyalty to the United Kingdom
I will uphold its democratic values
I will observe its laws faithfully

explanation_en: Long live our noble King! is the second sentence of the National Anthem of the UK.

question_en: Which of the following is NOT protected by the laws of discrimination?
explanation_en: UK laws ensure that people are not treated unfairly in any area of life or work because of their age, disability, sex, pregnancy and maternity, race, religion or belief, sexuality or marital status.

question_en: Which Act abolished the old pocket and rotten boroughs and allowed more parliamentary seats to the towns and cities?
explanation_en: The Reform Act of 1832 had greatly increased the number of people with the right to vote. The act also abolished the old pocket and rotten boroughs and more parliamentary seats were given to the towns and cities.

question_en: In the UK, if the jury finds a defendant guilty, the judge decides the penalty:
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: Which of the following countries is a member of the Commonwealth?
explanation_en: Bangladesh is a member of the Commonwealth.

question_en: During the Crusades, European Christians fought for the control of:
explanation_en: European Christians fought for control of the Holy Land .

question_en: The line ‘to be or not to be’ belongs to which of the following plays or poems written by William Shakespeare?
explanation_en: ‘To be or not to be’ is a line from William Shakespeare’s play Hamlet .

question_en: Charles I wanted the worship of the Church of England to include more ceremony and introduced a revised Prayer Book.
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: Which of the following is NOT a principle included in the European Convention of Human Rights?

options.en: Prohibition of slavery and forced labour

explanation_en: Prohibition of immoral thoughts is not one of the principles included in the European Convention of Human Rights.

question_en: What was the name of the project through which the atomic bomb was developed?
explanation_en: Scientists led by Ernest Rutherford, working at Manchester and then Cambridge University, were the first to ‘split the atom’ and took part in the Manhattan Project in the United States, which developed the atomic bomb.

question_en: Who supported the Parliament during the Civil War that began in 1642?
explanation_en: A civil war between the king and Parliament began in 1642. The country split into those who supported the king (the Cavaliers) and those who supported Parliament (the Roundheads) .

question_en: Which of the following poets wrote poems inspired by Scotland and the traditional stories and songs from the area on the borders of Scotland and England?
explanation_en: Sir Walter Scot t wrote poems inspired by Scotland and the traditional stories and songs from the area on the borders of Scotland and England

question_en: A system of government where the whole adult population gets a say is known as:
explanation_en: Democracy is a system of government where the whole adult population gets a say. This might be by direct voting or by choosing representatives to make decisions on their behalf.

question_en: Which of the following statements is correct?

options.en: Arranged marriages refer to marriages where both parties agree to the marriage
Arranged marriages refer to marriages where one or both parties do not give their consent to enter into the partnership

explanation_en: Arranged marriages refer to marriages where both parties agree to the marriage .

question_en: The devolved governments in Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland have the power to legislate on any issues that affect them:
explanation_en: Devolved governments in Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland have the power to legislate on certain issues .

question_en: What name is given to the members of the House of Commons?
explanation_en: All of the elected MPs form the House of Commons.

question_en: Which golf ‘Major’ tournament is held outside the United States?
explanation_en: The open championship is the only ‘Major’ tournament held outside the United States.

question_en: As part of the citizenship ceremony, new citizens pledge their loyalty to the United Kingdom and to respect its rights and freedoms.
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: Which of the following countries is NOT a member of the Commonwealth?
explanation_en: Greece is not a member of the Commonwealth.

question_en: The Welsh government has 30 Senedd members.
explanation_en: The Welsh government has 60 Senedd members .

question_en: Who was the first person to print books using a printing press in England?
explanation_en: William Caxton was the first person in England to print books using a printing press.

question_en: In England, Wales and Scotland, most minor criminal cases are dealt with in a Magistrates’ Court:
explanation_en: In England, Wales and Northern Ireland , most minor criminal cases are dealt with in a Magistrates’ Court. In Scotland, minor criminal offences go to a Justice of the Peace Court.

question_en: When did the UK sign the European Convention of Human Rights?
explanation_en: The UK was one of the first countries to sign the Convention in 1950 .

question_en: Which is the most famous rugby union competition?
explanation_en: The most famous rugby union competition is the Six Nations Championship between England, Ireland, Scotland, Wales, France and Italy.

question_en: After Oliver Cromwell defeated the Scottish army in the Battles of Dunbar and Worcester, Charles II escaped from Worcester, famously hiding in an oak tree on one occasion, and eventually fled to Europe:
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: Which of the following is an ancient festival and has roots in the pagan festival to mark the beginning of winter?
explanation_en: Halloween , 31 October, is and ancient festival and has roots in the pagan festival to mark the beginning of winter.

question_en: Which TWO British kings believed in the ‘Divine Right of Kings’?
explanation_en: James I and his son Charles I both believed in the ‘Divine Right of Kings’: the idea that the king was directly appointed by God to rule. They thought that the king should be able to act without having to seek approval from Parliament.

question_en: What was the name of the European Union when it was first established?
explanation_en: West Germany, France, Belgium, Italy, Luxembourg and the Netherlands formed the European Economic Community (EEC) in 1957. The EEC became a part of the European Union when it was formed in 1993.

question_en: By 1200, the English ruled an area of Scotland known as the Pale, around Edinburgh:
explanation_en: By 1200, the English ruled an area of Ireland known as the Pale, around Dublin .

question_en: Which of the following British poets was inspired by nature?
explanation_en: William Wordsworth was inspired by nature.

question_en: Which Act incorporated the European Convention of Human Rights into UK law?
explanation_en: The Human Rights Act 1998 incorporated the European Convention of Human Rights into UK law.

question_en: After the Second World War, the UK was exhausted economically.
explanation_en: Although the UK had won the war, the country was exhausted economically after WWII.

question_en: What percentage of the total British population is located in Northern Ireland?
explanation_en: The population is very unequally distributed over the four parts of the UK. England more or less consistently makes up 84% of the total population, Wales around 5%, Scotland just over 8%, and Northern Ireland less than 3% .

question_en: Bonfire Night remembers the day when a group of Catholics led by Guy Fawkes killed the Protestant king with a bomb in the Houses of Parliament:
explanation_en: Bonfire Night remembers the day when a group of Catholics led by Guy Fawkes failed in their plan to kill the Protestant king with a bomb in the Houses of Parliament.

question_en: Who was Florence Nightingale?

options.en: A nurse who worked in military hospitals, treating soldiers who were fighting in the Crimean War
One of the tribal leaders who fought against the Romans
A scientist who proposed that enemy aircraft could be detected by radio waves
A mathematician who was influential in the development of computer science

explanation_en: Florence Nightingale was a nurse who worked in military hospitals, treating soldiers who were fighting in the Crimean War.

question_en: Where is the Scottish Grand National horse racing event celebrated?
explanation_en: The Scottish Grand National horse racing event is celebrated at Ayr .

question_en: Arranged marriages are not acceptable in the UK:
explanation_en: Arranged marriages , where both parties agree to the marriage, are acceptable in the UK .

question_en: Who was the Prime Minister of the UK from 1945 to 1951?
explanation_en: Clement Attlee was Prime Minister from 1945 to 1951 and led the Labour Party for 20 years.

question_en: Who represents the UK to the rest of the world?
explanation_en: The King represents the UK to the rest of the world.

question_en: Great Britain refers only to England, Scotland and Wales, not to Northern Ireland.
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: Queen Elizabeth I was a Catholic:
explanation_en: Queen Elizabeth I was a Protestant .

question_en: What is the meaning of the term the ‘Divine Right of Kings’?

options.en: The idea that the king was appointed by landowners
The Parliament and the King shared responsibilities
The King could rule the country but with the support of the Parliament
The idea that the king was directly appointed by God to rule

explanation_en: The ‘Divine Right of Kings’ refers to the idea that the king was directly appointed by God to rule and that the king should be able to act without having to seek approval from Parliament.

question_en: Who was the first person to lead a Roman invasion in Britain in 55 BC?
explanation_en: Julius Caesar led a Roman invasion of Britain in 55 BC. This was unsuccessful and for nearly 100 years Britain remained separate from the Roman Empire.

question_en: Which of the following is NOT the responsibility of the MPs?

options.en: Scrutinise and comment on what the government is doing

explanation_en: MPs have a number of different responsibilities. They represent everyone in their constituency, help to create new laws, scrutinize and comment on what the government is doing and debate important national issues.

question_en: When Henry VIII took the title ‘King of Ireland’, English laws were introduced and everyone except for local leaders were expected to follow the instructions of the Lord Lieutenants in Dublin:
explanation_en: When Henry VIII took the title ‘King of Ireland’, English laws were introduced and local leaders were expected to follow the instructions of the Lord Lieutenants in Dublin.

question_en: When is the anniversary of the Battle of the Boyne celebrated in Northern Ireland?
explanation_en: In Northern Ireland, the anniversary of the Battle of the Boyne is celebrated in July and is also a public holiday.

question_en: The clock tower is named ‘Elizabeth Tower’ in honour of ___________.

options.en: Queen Elizabeth II’s Diamond Jubilee in 2012
Queen Elizabeth I’s Diamond Jubilee in 2012
Queen Elizabeth II’s Golden Jubilee in 2002
Queen Elizabeth I’s Golden Jubilee in 2002

explanation_en: The clock tower is named ‘Elizabeth Tower’ in honour of Queen Elizabeth II’s Diamond Jubilee in 2012 .

question_en: When was Lincoln Cathedral built?
explanation_en: Lincoln Cathedral was built in the middle ages or medieval times .

question_en: Which of the following territories forms part of the UK?
explanation_en: The UK is formed by England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland .

question_en: How old was Edward VI when he died?
explanation_en: Edward VI died at the age of 15 after ruling for just over six years, and his half-sister Mary became queen.

question_en: What name was given to those who supported the king during the Civil War that began in 1642?
explanation_en: During the Civil War that started in 1642 those who supported the king (the Cavaliers) and those who supported Parliament (the Roundheads).

question_en: The right to a fair trial is not included amongst the principles of the European Convention of Human Rights:
explanation_en: The right to a fair trial is one of the principles of the European Convention of Human Rights .

question_en: Why was Queen Mary known as ‘Bloody Mary’?

options.en: She used to drink a lot
Because of all the blood from the wars she caused
She was a devout Catholic and persecuted Protestants

explanation_en: Queen Mary was a devout Catholic and persecuted Protestants (for this reason, she became known as ‘Bloody Mary’).

question_en: Which of the following sentences is true?

options.en: The UK is governed by the parliament sitting in Edinburgh.
Scotland and Wales have parliaments or assemblies of their own, but not Northern Ireland.
Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland also have parliaments or assemblies of their own, with devolved powers in defined areas.
The UK is governed by the parliament sitting in Glasgow.

explanation_en: Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland also have parliaments or assemblies of their own, with devolved powers in defined areas.

question_en: Which of the following lines from Shakespeare’s plays and poems is often still quoted?
explanation_en: ‘All the world’s a stage’ is a line from William Shakespeare’s play As You Like It.

question_en: How many volunteers did the National Trust have when it first was formed in 1895?
explanation_en: The National Trust was founded in 1895 by three volunteers .

question_en: In the UK, brutality and violence in the home is not a crime:
explanation_en: In the UK, brutality and violence in the home is a serious crime .

question_en: When did modern tennis evolve in England?
explanation_en: Modern tennis evolved in England in the late 19th century .

question_en: UK laws ensure that people are not treated unfairly in any area of life or work because of their age, disability, sex, pregnancy and maternity, race, religion or belief, sexuality or marital status:
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: The population is very equally distributed over the four parts of the UK:
explanation_en: The population is very unequally distributed over the four parts of the UK. England more or less consistently makes up 84% of the total population, Wales around 5%, Scotland just over 8%, and Northern Ireland less than 3%.

question_en: Where was Anne Boleyn executed?

options.en: At the Houses of the Parliament

explanation_en: Anne Boleyn was executed at the tower of London .

question_en: At the beginning of the Middle Ages, Ireland was not an independent country:
explanation_en: At the beginning of the Middle Ages, Ireland was an independent country .

question_en: Since 1997, some powers have been devolved from the central government to give people in Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland more control over matters that directly affect them:
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: Where did Mary Stuart, the Queen of Scotland, spend most of her childhood?
explanation_en: Mary Stuart, the Queen of Scotland, spent most of her childhood in France .

question_en: During the Middle Ages, several cathedrals had windows of stained glass, telling stories about:

options.en: The journey of pilgrims to Canterbury

explanation_en: During the middle ages, several cathedrals had windows of stained glass, telling stories about the Bible and Christian saints . The glass in York Minster is a famous example.

question_en: Where does the UK Parliament sit?
explanation_en: The UK is governed by the parliament sitting in Westminster .

question_en: When did King Edward I of England introduced the Statute of Rhuddlan by which Wales annexed to the Crown of England?
explanation_en: In 1284 King Edward I of England introduced the Statute of Rhuddlan, which annexed Wales to the Crown of England.

question_en: Which of the following public services can be controlled by the devolved administrations?
explanation_en: Some public services, such as education , are controlled by the devolved administrations.

question_en: The Senedd has been suspended on a few occasions:
explanation_en: The Northern Ireland Assembly has been suspended on a few occasions.

question_en: The settlements of Scottish and English Protestants in Ulster (the northern province of Ireland) during the reigns of Elizabeth I and James I, who took over the land from Catholic landholders is known as:
explanation_en: These settlements were known as plantations .

question_en: Which of the following jobs is usually unpaid in England, Wales and Scotland?

options.en: Magistrates and Justices of the Peace

explanation_en: In England, Wales and Scotland, Magistrates and Justices of the Peace (JPs) are members of the local community and they usually work unpaid and do not need legal qualifications.

question_en: Which of the following is a traditional character of the pantomimes?
explanation_en: One of the traditional characters of the pantomimes is the Dame , a woman played by a man.

question_en: In the UK, most people live in towns and cities and very small parts of Britain are countryside:
explanation_en: False . Most people live in towns and cities but much of Britain is still countryside.

question_en: What was the symbol of Lancaster during the Wars of the Roses?
explanation_en: During the Wars of the Roses, the symbol of Lancaster was a red rose .

question_en: After King Alfred the Great defeated the Vikings, many of the Viking invaders stayed in Britain, especially in the east and north of England in an area called:
explanation_en: Many of the Viking invaders stayed in Britain – especially in the east and north of England in an area known as the Danelaw .

question_en: The Reform Act of 1832 increased the number of people with the right to vote:
explanation_en: True . The Reform Act of 1832 had greatly increased the number of people with the right to vote.

question_en: Which of the following is a famous example of stained glass?
explanation_en: York Minster is a famous example of stained glass used on the windows of some cathedrals during the middle ages.

question_en: Who is the head of the Church of England?
explanation_en: The monarch is the head of the Church of England.

question_en: Who is responsible for managing relationships with foreign countries?
explanation_en: The Foreign Secretary is responsible for managing relationships with foreign countries.

question_en: Which TWO of the following poems are from the Middle Ages?

options.en: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight

explanation_en: Poems which survive from the Middle Ages include Chaucer’s Canterbury Tales and a poem called Sir Gawain and the Green Knight , about one of the knights at the court of King Arthur.

question_en: If a husband forces his wife to have sex, he can be charged with rape:
explanation_en: True . Any man who forces a woman to have sex, including a woman’s husband, can be charged with rape.

question_en: Who defeated the English at the Battle of Bannockburn in 1314?
explanation_en: In 1314 the Scottish, led by Robert the Bruce , defeated the English at the Battle of Bannockburn.

question_en: In the UK, owners are responsible for keeping their dog under control and for cleaning up after the animal in public places:
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: How long did the Hundred Years War fought between England and France last for?
explanation_en: English kings fought a long war with France, called the Hundred Years War (even though it actually lasted 116 years ).

question_en: When did Gordon Brown take over as Prime Minister from his predecessor Tony Blair?
explanation_en: Gordon Brown took over as Prime Minister from Tony Blair in 2007 .

question_en: The Life in the UK test is usually taken is English, although special arrangements can be made if you wish to take it in French.
explanation_en: False . The Life in the UK test is usually taken is English, although special arrangements can be made if you wish to take it in Welsh or Scottish Gaelic.

question_en: Where in England was one of the Anglo-Saxon kings buried with treasure and armour?
explanation_en: The burial place of one of the Anglo-Saxon kings was at Sutton Hoo in modern Suffolk .

question_en: Which British writer wrote satirical novels including Brideshead Revisited?
explanation_en: Evelyn Waugh wrote satirical novels, including Decline and Fall and Scoop. He is perhaps best known for Brideshead Revisited.

question_en: Where is the best preserved prehistoric village in northern Europe located?

options.en: Edinburgh, the capital of the Scotland
Orkney, off the north coast of Scotland

explanation_en: Skara Brae on Orkney, off the north coast of Scotland , is the best preserved prehistoric village in northern Europe.

question_en: Which of the following sports began in the UK?
explanation_en: Many famous sports, including cricket, football, lawn tennis, golf and rugby , began in Britain.

question_en: Which famous architect designed Dumfries House?
explanation_en: The Scottish architect Robert Adam designed the inside decoration as well as the building itself in great houses such as Dumfries House in Scotland.

question_en: What did the Education Act of 1944 introduce?

options.en: Free primary education in England and Wales
Free secondary education in England and Wales
Free high school education in England and Wales
Free university education in England and Wales

explanation_en: The Education Act 1944 (often called ‘The Butler Act’), introduced free secondary education in England and Wales .

question_en: Which of the following tribes invaded Britain?
explanation_en: The Roman army left Britain in AD 410 to defend other parts of the Roman Empire and never returned. Britain was again invaded by tribes from northern Europe: the Jutes, the Angles and the Saxons .

question_en: Many schools organise events such as book sales, toy sales or bringing food to sell to raise money for extra equipment or out-of-school activities.
explanation_en: This statement is TRUE .

question_en: Do you need a TV licence for watching TV on a mobile phone?
explanation_en: Everyone in the UK with a TV, computer or other medium which can be used for watching TV must have a television licence.

question_en: What do you need to apply to become a British citizen?

options.en: Have lived in the UK for 3 years
Have a good understanding of life in the UK

explanation_en: To apply to become a permanent resident or citizen of the UK, you will need to have a good understanding of life in the UK .

question_en: Which of the following is a British overseas territory located in the South Atlantic?
explanation_en: The Falkland Islands , a British overseas territory is located in the South Atlantic.

question_en: Who wrote the novel Charlie and the Chocolate Factory?
explanation_en: Roald Dahl is the author of Charlie and the Chocolate Factory.

question_en: The Normans used a system of land ownership known as __________.
explanation_en: The Normans used a system of land ownership known as feudalism .

question_en: Who were the Huguenots?
explanation_en: Between 1680 and 1720 many refugees called Huguenots came from France . They were Protestants and had been persecuted for their religion. Many were educated and skilled and worked as scientists, in banking, or in weaving or other crafts.

question_en: Which of the following statements is TRUE?

options.en: The official name of the country is the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Southern Ireland.
The official name of the country is Great Britain and Southern Ireland.
The official name of the country is the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland.
The official name of the country is the United Kingdom and Great Ireland.

explanation_en: The official name of the country is the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland.

question_en: When was slavery abolished throughout the British Empire?
explanation_en: In 1833 the Emancipation Act abolished slavery throughout the British Empire.

question_en: Magistrates and Justices of the Peace (JPs) are members of the _________.
explanation_en: Magistrates and Justices of the Peace (JPs) are members of the local community .

question_en: Where can you find details about the small claims procedure?

options.en: At your local County Court or Sheriff Court

explanation_en: You can get details about the small claims procedure from your local County Court or Sheriff Court .

question_en: When is Mothering Sunday?

options.en: The Sunday three weeks before Easter.
The Sunday three weeks after Easter.
The Sunday three weeks before Christmas.
The Sunday three weeks after Christmas.

explanation_en: Mothering Sunday (or Mother’s day) is the Sunday three weeks before Easter . Children send cards or buy gifts for their mothers.

question_en: What should you do if your vehicle is over three years old?

options.en: Take it to the MOT every year.
Take it to the MOT 2 years.
Take it to the MOT 3 years.
Take it to the MOT six months.

explanation_en: If you vehicle is over three years old, you must take it to the Ministry of Transport (MOT) test every year .

question_en: Which countries fought alongside Britain against Russia during the Crimean War?
explanation_en: From 1853 to 1856, Britain fought with Turkey and France against Russia in the Crimean War.

question_en: On average, boys leave school with better qualifications than girls.
explanation_en: False . On average, girls leave school with better qualifications than boys.

question_en: When did William of Orange invade England to proclaim himself king?
explanation_en: In 1688 , important Protestants in England asked William of Orange to invade England and proclaim himself king. When William reached England, there was no resistance. James fled to France and William took over the throne, becoming William III in England, Wales and Ireland, and William II of Scotland.

question_en: Which British painter is considered to be the artist who raised the profile of landscape painting?
explanation_en: Joseph Turner (1775-1851) was an influential landscape painter in a modern style. He is considered the artist who raised the profile of landscape painting.

question_en: The development of the Bessemer process during the Industrial Revolution led to the development of which two industries:
explanation_en: The development of the Bessemer process for the mass production of steel led to the development of the shipbuilding industry and the railways .

question_en: What is the length of the Bayeux tapestry?
explanation_en: The Bayeux Tapestry is nearly 70 metres (230 feet) long and is embroidered with coloured wool.

question_en: Which of the following countries is not a member of the Commonwealth?
explanation_en: Costa Rica is not a member of the Commonwealth.

question_en: In which of the following years did England win the World Cup?
explanation_en: England won the World Cup in 1966 when it was captained by Bobby Moore.

question_en: During the Battle of Trafalgar Britain fought against:
explanation_en: Britain’s navy fought against combined French and Spanish fleets , winning the Battle of Trafalgar in 1805.

question_en: What is the name given to the first formal anti-slavery groups set up in the late 1700s?
explanation_en: The first formal anti-slavery groups were set up by the Quakers in the late 1700s, and they petitioned Parliament to ban the practice.

question_en: More women than men study at university.
explanation_en: This statement is TRUE .

question_en: Why did Ireland suffer a famine in the middle of the 19th century?
explanation_en: In the middle of the century the potato crop failed , and Ireland suffered a famine. A million people died from disease and starvation.

question_en: What sort of battle was ‘the Battle of Britain’?

options.en: A naval battle against the Spanish.
An aerial battle against the French.
An aerial battle against the Germans.
A naval battle against the French.

explanation_en: Hitler wanted to invade Britain, but before sending in troops, Germany needed to control the air campaign against Britain, but the British resisted with their fighter planes and eventually won the crucial aerial battle against the Germans , called ‘the Battle of Britain’, in the summer of 1940.

question_en: Who is in charge of appointing the local Chief Constable?
explanation_en: PCCs (Police and Crime Commissioners) set local police priorities and the local policing budget. They also appoint the local Chief Constable.

question_en: What does the money from the National Insurance Contributions pay for (CHOOSE TWO)?
explanation_en: The money raised from National Insurance Contributions is used to pay for state benefits and services such as the state retirement pension and the National Health Service (NHS) .

question_en: How many British died on the first day alone of the Battle of Somme?
explanation_en: The British attack of the Somme in July 1916, resulted in about 60,000 British casualties on the first day alone.

question_en: When was the first Union flag created?
explanation_en: The first Union Flag was created in 1606 from the flags of Scotland and England, the Principality of Wales was already untied with England.

question_en: Which of the following territories is not part of the UK?
explanation_en: The UK comprises England, Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland. There are several islands which are closely linked with the UK but are not part of it: the Channel Islands and the Isle of Man .

question_en: Which of the following is NOT a fundamental principle of British life?
explanation_en: The fundamental principles of British life include: democracy, the rule of law, individual liberty, tolerance of those with different faiths and beliefs and participation in community life.

question_en: What do Sir Terence Conran, Clarice Cliff and Thomas Chippendale have in common?
explanation_en: Britain has produced many great designers , from Thomas Chippendale (who designed furniture in the 18th century) to Clarice Cliff (who designed Art Deco ceramics) to Sir Terence Conran (a 20th-century interior designer).

question_en: Which of the following cities is not in Wales?
explanation_en: Bradford is located in England.

question_en: People on the electoral register with ages between 18 and 70 can be asked to serve on a jury.
explanation_en: True . As well as getting the right to vote, people on the electoral register are randomly selected to serve on a jury. Anyone who is on the electoral register and is aged 18 to 70 can be asked to do this.

question_en: Which Saxon king of England was killed at the Battle of Hastings in 1066?
explanation_en: In 1066, an invasion led by William, the Duke of Normandy (in what is now northern France), defeated Harold , the Saxon king of England, at the Battle of Hastings. Harold was killed in the battle.

question_en: During the ‘swinging sixties’ Britain experienced a growth in:

options.en: Sports such as rowing and cycling

explanation_en: The decade of the 1960s was a period of significant social change. It was known as the ‘swinging sixties’. There was growth in British fashion, cinema and popular music .

question_en: As a permanent resident or citizen of the UK you should:

options.en: Look after yourself and your family
Look after the area in which you live and the environment

explanation_en: If you wish to be a permanent resident or citizen of the UK, you should: respect and obey the law, respect the rights of others, including their right to their own opinions, treat others with fairness, look after yourself and your family and look after the area in which you live and the environment.

question_en: When did the BBC start the world’s first regular television service?
explanation_en: The BBC started radio broadcasts in 1922 and began the world’s first regular television service in 1936 .

question_en: Women in Britain today make up about one third of the workforce.
explanation_en: Women in Britain today make up about half of the workforce .

question_en: The evacuation of British and French soldiers from France in a huge naval operation during WWII gave rise to which phrase:
explanation_en: During WWII as France fell, the British decided to evacuate British and French soldiers from France in a huge naval operation. Many civilian volunteers in small pleasure and fishing boats from Britain helped the Navy to rescue more than 300,000 men from the beaches around Dunkirk. The evacuation gave rise to the phrase ‘the Dunkirk spirit’ .

question_en: Which programme helps 16 and 17 year-olds develop their skills and are part in community projects?
explanation_en: The National Citizen Service programme gives 16- and 17-year-olds the opportunity to enjoy outdoor activities, develop their skills and take part in a community project.

question_en: Queen Victoria became queen in:
explanation_en: In 1837 , Queen Victoria became queen of the UK at the age of 18.

question_en: Who wrote the books ‘Pride and Prejudice’ and ‘Sense and Sensibility’?
explanation_en: Jane Austen (1775-1817) was an English novelist. Her books include Pride and Prejudice and Sense and Sensibility.

question_en: Which political party did Margaret Thatcher belong to?
explanation_en: Margaret Thatcher led the Conservative government from 1979 to 1990.

question_en: In which city is the Millennium Stadium located?
explanation_en: The Millennium Stadium is located in Cardiff .

question_en: Who lost a lot of power and influence after the Battle of Culloden in 1746?
explanation_en: The clans lost a lot of their power and influence after Culloden. Chieftains became landlords if they had the favour of the English king, and clansmen became tenants who had to pay for the land they used.

question_en: What UK landmark was voted as Britain’s favourite view in 2007?

options.en: The Crown Jewels at the Tower of London

explanation_en: In 2007, television viewers voted Wastwater as Britain’s favourite view.

question_en: When did the development of a free press take place?
explanation_en: From 1695 , newspapers were allowed to operate without a government licence. Increasing numbers of newspapers began to be published.

question_en: Which was the last successful foreign invasion of England?
explanation_en: The Norman Conquest was the last successful foreign invasion of England and led to many changes in government and social structures in England.

question_en: Who was Richard Austen Butler?
explanation_en: Richard Austen Butler (later Lord Butler) became a Conservative MP in 1923 and held several positions before becoming responsible for education in 1941.

question_en: What kind of sport is played at the Wimbledon Championships?
explanation_en: The most famous tennis tournament hosted in Britain is The Wimbledon Championships, which takes place each year at the All England Lawn Tennis and Croquet Club.

question_en: Where was Isaac Newton from?
explanation_en: Isaac Newton was born in Lincolnshire, eastern England .

question_en: Under which Act was The Kingdom of Great Britain created?
explanation_en: The Act of Union , known as the Treaty of Union in Scotland, was therefore agreed in 1707, creating the Kingdom of Great Britain.

question_en: The Council of Europe has the power to make new laws.
explanation_en: False . The Council of Europe has NO power to make laws but draws up conventions and charters , the most well-known of which is the European Convention on Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms, usually called the European Convention on Human Rights.

question_en: Which TWO British poets wrote about their experiences in the WWI?

options.en: John Keats and Alfred Lord Tennyson

explanation_en: Wilfred Owen and Siegfried Sassoon were inspired to write about their experiences in the First World War.

question_en: If you are found driving while exceeding the alcohol limit you will:

options.en: Not be allowed to drive again
Be taken home by the police

explanation_en: If you are found driving while exceeding the alcohol limit you will be arrested .

question_en: Which TWO of the following are core values of a civil servant?
explanation_en: The core values of civil servants include: integrity, honesty, objectivity and impartiality (including being politically neutral).

question_en: Which of the following countries belongs to the Commonwealth?
explanation_en: Nauru is a member of the Commonwealth.

question_en: What is the name of the first Danish king to rule in England?
explanation_en: Anglo-Saxon kings continued to rule what is now England, except for a short period when there were Danish kings. The first of these was Cnut, also named Canute .

question_en: What name was given to the members of the Women’s Social and Political Union founded in 1903?
explanation_en: In 1903 Emmeline Pankhurst helped found the Women’s Social and Political Union (WSPU). This was the first group whose members were called ‘suffragettes’ .

question_en: It is a civil offence to owe money to someone and you might be taken to court.
explanation_en: True . Owing money to someone is a civil offence.

question_en: In which country of the British Empire did the Boer War take place?
explanation_en: The Boer War of 1899 to 1902 made the discussions about the future of the Empire more urgent. The British went to war in South Africa with settlers from the Netherlands called the Boers.

question_en: Robert Louis Stevenson, Graham Greene and Sir Kingsley Amis were British writers.
explanation_en: This statement is True .

question_en: Which new industries developed in the UK during the Great Depression (choose 2 answers)?
explanation_en: In 1929, the world entered the ‘Great Depression’ and some parts of the UK suffered mass unemployment. The effects of the depression of the 1930s were felt differently in different parts of the UK. The traditional heavy industries such as shipbuilding were badly affected but new industries – including the automobile and aviation industries – developed.

question_en: Which of the following is a traditional pub game?
explanation_en: Pool and darts are traditional pub games.

question_en: When was the Battle of Agincourt fought?
explanation_en: One of the most famous battles of the Hundred Years War was the Battle of Agincourt in 1415 , where King Henry V’s vastly outnumbered English army defeated the French. The English left France in the 1450s.

question_en: What is the Bessemer process?

options.en: An industrial process for the mass-production of iron
An industrial process for the mass-production of copper
An industrial process for the mass-production of titanium
An industrial process for the mass-production of steel

explanation_en: The development of the Bessemer process for the mass production of steel led to the development of the shipbuilding industry and the railways.

question_en: When did people learn to make bronze?
explanation_en: Around 4,000 years ago, people learned to make bronze. We call this period the Bronze Age.

question_en: When is the Jewish celebration known as Hanukkah normally celebrated?
explanation_en: Hanukkah is in November or December and is celebrated for eight days.

question_en: What musical award is given during the Mercury Music Prize?

options.en: Best album from the UK and Ireland
Best solo artist from the UK
Best solo artist the UK and Ireland

explanation_en: The Mercury Music Prize is awarded each September for the best album from the UK and Ireland.

question_en: Who was Boudicca?

options.en: One of the tribal leaders who fought against the Romans
One of the architects who built Hadrian’s Wall
One of the tribal leaders who fought against the Vikings

explanation_en: One of the tribal leaders who fought against the Romans was Boudicca, the queen of the Iceni in what is now eastern England.

question_en: Which TWO of the following water sports are popular in the UK?
explanation_en: Sailing continues to be popular in the UK, reflecting its maritime heritage. Rowing is also popular, both as a leisure activity and as a competitive sport.

question_en: Which of the following statements is TRUE?

options.en: Forcing another person to marry is a civil offence
Forcing another person to marry is a criminal offence
Forcing another person to marry is not punished in the UK
Forcing another person to marry is common in the UK

explanation_en: Forcing another person to marry is a criminal offence.

question_en: In the UK, what should dogs wear in public places?

options.en: A collar with the name and address of the owner
A collar with the name of the dog
A tracking device in case they get lost
Clothes to avoid the cold in the winter

explanation_en: All dogs in public places must wear a collar showing the name and address of the owner.

question_en: Where do UK residents need to register their car or motorcycle?

options.en: At the Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA)

explanation_en: If you are resident in the UK, your car or motor cycle must be registered at the Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA).

question_en: Popular social networking websites in the UK include:
explanation_en: Social networking websites such as Facebook and Twitter are a popular in the UK.

question_en: According to the 2011 Census, what percentage of the population identified themselves as Muslim?
explanation_en: In the 2011 Census, 59% of people identified themselves as Christian. Much smaller proportions identified themselves as Muslim (4.8%), Hindu (1.5%), Sikh (0.8%), Jewish or Buddhist (both less than 0.5%).

question_en: How often are elections held for the Welsh government?
explanation_en: The Welsh government has 60 Senedd members (SMs) and elections are held every four years using a form of proportional representation.

question_en: Who was the British Minister after Margaret Thatcher?
explanation_en: John Major was Prime Minister after Mrs Thatcher, and helped establish the Northern Ireland peace process.

question_en: What name is given to the person appointed by local authorities to be the ceremonial leader of a particular council?
explanation_en: Many local authorities appoint a mayor , who is the ceremonial leader of the council. In some towns, a mayor is elected to be the effective leader of the administration.

question_en: What is the meaning of the term ‘first past the post’?

options.en: In each constituency, the candidate who gets the most votes is elected
If no political party wins a majority, two parties may join and govern together
If an MP dies or resigns, there will be a fresh election
The Speaker will be chosen by other MPs in a secret ballot

explanation_en: MPs are elected through a system called ‘first past the post’. In each constituency, the candidate who gets the most votes is elected.

question_en: Who defeated Emperor Napoleon at the Battle of Waterloo in 1815?
explanation_en: In 1815, the French Wars ended with the defeat of the Emperor Napoleon by the Duke of Wellington at the Battle of Waterloo.

question_en: Which court deals with the MOST serious cases in Scotland, such as murder?
explanation_en: In Scotland, serious cases are heard in a Sheriff Court with either a sheriff or a sheriff with a jury. The most serious cases in Scotland, such as murder, are heard at a High Court with a judge and jury.

question_en: What was the symbol of the House of Tudor?

options.en: A red rose with a white rose inside it
A white rose with a red rose inside it

explanation_en: The symbol of the House of Tudor was a red rose with a white rose inside it as a sign that the Houses of York and Lancaster were now allies.

question_en: What animal is represented in the Welsh flag?
explanation_en: There is also an official Welsh flag, which shows a Welsh dragon . The Welsh dragon does not appear on the Union Flag because, when the first Union Flag was created in 1606 from the flags of Scotland and England, the Principality of Wales was already untied with England.

question_en: Which famous economist developed ideas about economics during the 18th century and which are still referred to today?
explanation_en: During the 18th century, new ideas about politics, philosophy and science were developed. This is often called ‘the Enlightenment’. Many of the great thinkers of the Enlightenment were Scottish. Adam Smith developed ideas about economics which are still referred to today.

question_en: Who is the ceremonial head of the Commonwealth?

options.en: The President of the United States
The Prime Minister of the UK

explanation_en: The King is the ceremonial head of the Commonwealth.

question_en: What time do pubs usually open during the week?
explanation_en: Pubs are usually open during the day from 11.00 am (12 noon on Sundays).

question_en: In which TWO movie categories does Britain continue to be particularly strong at?
explanation_en: Britain continues to be particularly strong in special effects and animation movies.

question_en: Which British artist was a portrait painter who often painted people in country or garden scenarios?
explanation_en: Thomas Gainsborough (1727-88) was a portrait painter who often painted people in country or garden scenery.

question_en: During which religious celebration do Muslims sacrifice an animal to eat?
explanation_en: Many Muslims sacrifice an animal to eat during Eid ul Adha.

question_en: Who was the first Briton to win the ‘Tour de France’?
explanation_en: Sir Bradley Wiggins (1980-) was the first Briton to win the Tour de France.

question_en: Which of the following statements is TRUE?

options.en: The English language has no dialects
The English language has many accents and dialects
Gaelic is spoken in North Wales
In parts of Northern Ireland people speak French

question_en: How old do you need to be to go into betting shops or gambling clubs?
explanation_en: You have to be 18 to go into betting shops or gambling clubs.

question_en: What do Ian McEwan, Hilary Mantel and Julian Barnes have in common?

options.en: They were awarded with the Man Booker Prize for Fiction
They won a Nobel Prize in Literature
They were awarded with the Mercury Music Prize

explanation_en: The Man Booker Prize for Fiction is awarded annually for the best fiction novel written by an author from the Commonwealth, Ireland or Zimbabwe. It has been awarded since 1968. Past winners include Ian McEwan, Hilary Mantel and Julian Barnes.

question_en: Who was Henry Purcell?
explanation_en: Henry Purcell (1659-95) was the organist at Westminster Abbey . He wrote church music, operas and other pieces, and developed a British style distinct from that elsewhere in Europe.

question_en: Where is the Giant’s Causeway located?
explanation_en: The Giant’s Causeway is located on the north-east coast of Northern Ireland .

question_en: During Halloween people carve lanterns out of ______ and put a candle inside of them.
explanation_en: During Halloween people carve lanterns out of pumpkins and put a candle inside of them.

question_en: Royal Ascot is a four-day horse race meeting in Berkshire attended by members of the Royal Family:
explanation_en: Royal Ascot , a five-day race meeting in Berkshire attended by members of the Royal Family.

question_en: Which of the following statements regarding Scottish television is TRUE?

options.en: There is a channel with programmes in the Gaelic language
There are three channels in the Gaelic language
All programmes are broadcasted in English
There are no specific programmes to Scotland

explanation_en: In Scotland, some Scotland-specific programmes are shown and there is also a channel with programmes in the Gaelic language .

question_en: Where does the biggest and most well-known Fringe Festival take place?
explanation_en: The biggest and most well-known Fringe Festival is the Edinburgh Festival Fringe .

question_en: Who composed a suite of pieces themed around the planets and the solar system called ‘The Planets’?
explanation_en: More recently, important composers include Gustav Holst (1874-1934), whose work includes The Planets, a suite of pieces themed around the planets and the solar system.

question_en: What was the population of the UK in 1998?
explanation_en: The population of the UK in 1998 was 57 million people .

question_en: Which of the following is a musical venue located in London?
explanation_en: There are many large venues that host music events throughout the year, such as: Wembley Stadium; The O2 in Greenwich, south-east London.

question_en: Who directed the movie ‘The Killing Fields’ in 1984?
explanation_en: The Killing Fields (1984) was directed by Roland Joffé .

question_en: Which TWO of the following castles are located in Scotland?
explanation_en: Inveraray Castle and Crathes Castle are located in Scotland.

question_en: Where is the Grand National horse racing event celebrated?
explanation_en: The Grand National horse racing event is celebrated in Aintree near Liverpool .

question_en: What was Charles Chaplin famous for?
explanation_en: Sir Charles (Charlie) Chaplin became famous in silent movies for his tramp character and was one of many British actors to make a career in Hollywood.

question_en: What percentage of the British population has a parent or grandparent born outside of the UK as a result of post-war immigration?
explanation_en: Post-war immigration means that nearly 10% of the population has a parent or grandparent born outside the UK.

question_en: Which of the following categories is recognised at the Laurence Olivier Awards?
explanation_en: The Laurence Olivier Awards recognise achievements in the area of theatre .

question_en: In the UK, there is a National Lottery for which draws are made every month:
explanation_en: False . There is a National Lottery for which draws are made every week.

question_en: Where is Europe’s longest dry ski slope located?
explanation_en: There are five ski centres in Scotland, as well as Europe’s longest dry ski slope near Edinburgh .

question_en: What was the population of the UK in 1600?
explanation_en: The population of the UK in 1600 was just over 4 million people .

question_en: What name is given to the 31st December in Scotland?
explanation_en: In Scotland, the 31st of December is called Hogmanay .

question_en: When is the Sikh festival which celebrates the founding of the Sikh community (Khalsa) and known as Vaisakhi celebrated?
explanation_en: Vaisakhi (also spelled Baisakhi) is a Sikh festival which celebrates the founding of the Sikh community known as the Khalsa. It is celebrated on 14 April each year with parades, dancing and singing.

question_en: What is the name of the highest mountain in Wales?
explanation_en: Snowdon is the highest mountain in Wales.

question_en: How many pence are there in a pound?
explanation_en: There are 100 pence in a pound.

question_en: When did rugby originate?
explanation_en: Rugby originated in England in the early 19th century and is very popular in the UK today.

question_en: How many volunteers did the National Trust have when it was first formed in 1895?
explanation_en: The National Trust was founded in 1895 by three volunteers .

question_en: When did modern tennis evolve in England?
explanation_en: Modern tennis evolved in England in the late 19th century .

question_en: The population is very equally distributed over the four parts of the UK:
explanation_en: False . The population is very unequally distributed over the four parts of the UK. England more or less consistently makes up 84% of the total population, Wales around 5%, Scotland just over 8%, and Northern Ireland less than 3%.

question_en: The UK population is ethnically diverse and changing rapidly, especially in large cities such as London:
explanation_en: True . The UK population is ethnically diverse and changing rapidly, especially in large cities such as London.

question_en: What name is given to the celebration when people in Great Britain set off fireworks at home or in special displays on the 5th of November?
explanation_en: Bonfire Night , 5 November, is an occasion when people in Great Britain set off fireworks at home or in special displays.

question_en: How many National Parks are there in England, Wales and Scotland?
explanation_en: There are 15 national parks in England, Wales and Scotland. They are areas of protected countryside that everyone can visit, and where people live, work and look after the landscape.

question_en: What services can be obtained from veterinary surgeons?

options.en: They are places where pets can be left when you go on holidays
They are places where vaccinations and medical treatment for animals is available
They are places specifically prepare to groom pets

explanation_en: Vaccinations and medical treatment for animals are available from veterinary surgeons (vets).

question_en: What is the height of the London Eye?
explanation_en: The London Eye is situated on the southern bank of the River Thames and is a Ferris wheel that is 443 feet (135 metres) tall.

question_en: Which of the following British poets was inspired by nature?
explanation_en: William Wordsworth was inspired by nature.

question_en: What percentage of the total British population is located in Northern Ireland?
explanation_en: The population is very unequally distributed over the four parts of the UK. England more or less consistently makes up 84% of the total population, Wales around 5%, Scotland just over 8%, and Northern Ireland less than 3% .

question_en: Bonfire Night remembers the day when a group of Catholics led by Guy Fawkes killed the Protestant king with a bomb in the Houses of Parliament:
explanation_en: False . Bonfire Night remembers the day when a group of Catholics led by Guy Fawkes failed in their plan to kill the Protestant king with a bomb in the Houses of Parliament.

question_en: Where is the Scottish Grand National horse racing event celebrated?
explanation_en: The Scottish Grand National horse racing event is celebrated at Ayr .

question_en: How long does the Hanukkah celebration last for?
explanation_en: Hanukkah is in November or December and is celebrated for eight days .

question_en: What percentage of the total British population is located in Wales?
explanation_en: The total British population located in Wales is around 5% .

question_en: Which of the following movies played an important role in boosting morale during the Second World War?
explanation_en: During the Second World War, British movies (for example, In Which We Serve ) played an important part in boosting morale.

question_en: Who won the 2012 Olympic gold medal in the heptathlon?
explanation_en: Jessica Ennis (1986-) won the 2012 Olympic gold medal in the heptathlon, which includes seven different track and field events. She also holds a number of British athletics records.

question_en: What is the name of the annual cultural Welsh festival which includes music, dance, art and original performances largely in Welsh?

options.en: The National Performance Festival of Wales

explanation_en: The National Eisteddfod of Wales is an annual cultural festival which includes music, dance, art and original performances largely in Welsh. It includes a number of important competitions for Welsh poetry.

question_en: What is the most well-known rugby league (club) competition?
explanation_en: The Super League is the most well-known rugby league (club) competition.

question_en: Where do the Laurence Olivier Awards take place?
explanation_en: The Laurence Olivier Awards take place annually at different venues in London .

question_en: Which of the following is a traditional character of the pantomimes?
explanation_en: One of the traditional characters of the pantomimes is the Dame , a woman played by a man.

question_en: In the UK, most people live in towns and cities and very small parts of Britain are countryside:
explanation_en: False . Most people live in towns and cities but much of Britain is still countryside.

question_en: What percentage of the total British population is located in Scotland?
explanation_en: The population is very unequally distributed over the four parts of the UK. England more or less consistently makes up 84% of the total population, Wales around 5%, Scotland just over 8% , and Northern Ireland less than 3%.

question_en: Which of the following academy awards is the equivalent of the Oscars in the UK?
explanation_en: The annual British Academy Film Awards, hosted by the British Academy of Film and Television Arts (BAFTA) , are the British equivalent of the Oscars.

question_en: What is a bank holiday (choose TWO answers)?

options.en: A day when banks are closed

explanation_en: In the UK, there are public holidays each year called bank holidays, when banks and many other businesses are closed for the day.

question_en: Which of the following countries does not take part in the Six Nations Championship?
explanation_en: The most famous rugby union competition is the Six Nations Championship between England, Ireland, Scotland, Wales, France and Italy.

question_en: Who looks after Edinburgh Castle?
explanation_en: Edinburgh Castle is looked after by Historic Environment Scotland , a Scottish government agency.

question_en: Who were the pioneers of the IVF (in-vitro fertilisation) therapy?

options.en: Sir Frank Whittle and Sir Christopher Cockrell
Sir Robert Edwards and Patrick Steptoe
Francis Creek and John Logie Baird
Sir Ian Wilmot and Keith Campbell

explanation_en: IVF (in-vitro fertilisation) therapy for the treatment of infertility was pioneered in Britain by physiologist Sir Robert Edwards (1925-) and gynaecologist Patrick Steptoe (1913-88). The world’s first ‘test-tube baby’ was born in Oldham, Lancashire in 1978.

question_en: In the UK, bank holidays of no religious significance are celebrated at the beginning of May, in late May or early June, and in August:
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: What TWO different types of rugby are practised in the UK?
explanation_en: There are two different types of rugby, which have different rules: union and league .

question_en: What do William Blake, John Keats and Lord Byron have in common?

options.en: They were British poets of the 19th century
They were British painters of the Swinging Sixties
They were musicians of the middle ages

explanation_en: Poetry was very popular in the 19th century, with poets such as William Blake, John Keats, Lord Byron, Percy Shelley, Alfred Lord Tennyson, and Robert and Elizabeth Browning.

question_en: The Laurence Olivier Awards take place annually at different venues in Liverpool:
explanation_en: False . The Laurence Olivier Awards take place annually at different venues in London.

question_en: An additional rented piece of land where people can grow fruit and vegetables is known as:
explanation_en: In the UK, some people rent additional land called ‘an allotment’ , where they grow fruit and vegetables.

question_en: Which TWO pop music groups became popular in the 1960s?
explanation_en: Two well-known pop music groups in the 1960s were The Beatles and The Rolling Stones .

question_en: Eid ul Adha is the day when Muslims thank Allah for giving them the strength to complete the fast:
explanation_en: False . The day when Muslims thank Allah for giving them the strength to complete the fast is called Eid a-Fitr.

question_en: Which of the following poets wrote poems inspired by Scotland and the traditional stories and songs from the area on the borders of Scotland and England?
explanation_en: Sir Walter Scott wrote poems inspired by Scotland and the traditional stories and songs from the area on the borders of Scotland and England.

question_en: Which golf ‘Major’ tournament is held outside the United States?
explanation_en: The open championship is the only ‘Major’ tournament held outside the United States.

question_en: Which is the most famous rugby union competition?
explanation_en: The most famous rugby union competition is the Six Nations Championship between England, Ireland, Scotland, Wales, France and Italy.

question_en: Which of the following is an ancient festival and has roots in the pagan festival to mark the beginning of winter?
explanation_en: Halloween , 31 October, is and ancient festival and has roots in the pagan festival to mark the beginning of winter.

question_en: When is the anniversary of the Battle of the Boyne celebrated in Northern Ireland?
explanation_en: In Northern Ireland, the anniversary of the Battle of the Boyne is celebrated in July and is also a public holiday.

question_en: Who is the head of the Church of England?
explanation_en: The monarch is the head of the Church of England.

question_en: In the UK, owners are responsible for keeping their dog under control and for cleaning up after the animal in public places:
explanation_en: This statement is true .

question_en: Which British writer wrote satirical novels including ‘Brideshead Revisited’?
explanation_en: Evelyn Waugh wrote satirical novels, including Decline and Fall and Scoop. He is perhaps best known for Brideshead Revisited.

question_en: What was the population of the UK in 1851?
explanation_en: The population of the UK in 1851 was 20 million people .

question_en: Which book written by JRR Tolkien was voted the country’s best-loved novel in 2003?
explanation_en: In 2003, The Lord of the Rings by JRR Tolkien was voted the country’s best-loved novel.

question_en: What is the note with the highest value in the UK?
explanation_en: The note with the highest value in the UK is the £50 note.

question_en: What day does Lent start?
explanation_en: Lent begins on Ash Wednesday .

question_en: How old do you need to be to be able to apply for a free TV licence?
explanation_en: People over 75 can apply for a free TV licence and blind people can get a 50% discount.

question_en: What time do usually pubs open on Sundays in the UK?
explanation_en: Pubs are usually open during the day from 11.00 am ( 12 noon on Sundays ).

question_en: Which British sportswoman won two gold medals for running in the 2004 Olympic Games?
explanation_en: Dame Kelly Holmes won two gold medals for running in the 2004 Olympic Games. She has held a number of British and European records.

question_en: What percentage of the total British population is located in England alone?
explanation_en: The population is very unequally distributed over the four parts of the UK. England more or less consistently makes up 84% of the total population, Wales around 5%, Scotland just over 8%, and Northern Ireland less than 3%.

question_en: What was the population of the UK in 2010?
explanation_en: The population of the UK in 2010 was just over 62 million people .

question_en: Which flower is associated with England?
explanation_en: The rose is the flower associated with England.

question_en: In which period did British Film studios flourish?
explanation_en: British film studios flourished in the 1930s .

question_en: According to the 2011 Census, what percentage of the population identified themselves as Sikh?
explanation_en: According to the 2011 Census, 0.8% of the population identified themselves as Sikh.

question_en: Which flower is associated with Wales?
explanation_en: The daffodil is the flower associated with Wales.

question_en: Which of the following is NOT a city of the UK?
explanation_en: Dublin is not a city of the UK.

question_en: When is Bonfire Night celebrated?
explanation_en: Bonfire Night is celebrated on the 5th of November .

question_en: Which of the following statements regarding television in Northern Ireland is TRUE?

options.en: Some programmes are broadcasted in Irish Gaelic
All programmes are broadcasted in English
There are no specific programmes to Northern Ireland

explanation_en: There are programmes specific to Northern Ireland and some programmes broadcast in Irish Gaelic .

question_en: In the second half of the 19th century there was an important group of artists who painted detailed pictures on religious or literary themes in bright colours. These were known as:
explanation_en: The Pre-Raphaelites were an important group of artists in the second half of the 19th century. They painted detailed pictures on religious or literary themes in bright colours. The group included Holman Hunt, Dante Gabriel Rossetti and Sir John Millais.

question_en: The Muslim festival known as Eid ul Adha reminds Muslims of their own commitment to God:
explanation_en: True . Eid ul Adha reminds Muslims of their own commitment to God.

question_en: What was the population of the UK in 1901?
explanation_en: The population of the UK in 1901 was 40 million people .

question_en: When is Boxing Day celebrated?
explanation_en: Boxing Day is the day after Christmas Day and is celebrated on the 26th December .

question_en: Which charity works for the preservation of buildings in England?
explanation_en: In the UK, many parts of the countryside and places of interest are kept open by the National Trust in England, Wales and Northern Ireland and the National Trust for Scotland. Both are charities that work to preserve important buildings, coastline and countryside in the UK.

question_en: Which of the following is a traditional Welsh food?
explanation_en: Welsh cakes are a traditional Welsh snack made from flour, dried fruits and spices, and served either hot or cold.

question_en: When is the Jewish celebration known as Hanukkah normally celebrated?
explanation_en: Hannukah is in November or December and is celebrated for eight days.

question_en: What musical award is given during the Mercury Music Prize?

options.en: Best album from the UK and Ireland
Best solo artist from the UK
Best solo artist the UK and Ireland

explanation_en: The Mercury Music Prize is awarded each September for the best album from the UK and Ireland .

question_en: Which TWO of the following water sports are popular in the UK?
explanation_en: Sailing continues to be popular in the UK, reflecting our maritime heritage. Rowing is also popular, both as a leisure activity and as a competitive sport.

question_en: In the UK, what should dogs wear in public places?

options.en: A collar with the name and address of the owner
A collar with the name of the dog
A tracking device in case they get lost
Clothes to avoid the cold in the winter

explanation_en: All dogs in public places must wear a collar showing the name and address of the owner .

question_en: According to the Citizenship Survey carried out in the UK in 2009, what percentage of the population identified themselves as Muslim?
explanation_en: In the 2009 Citizenship Survey, 70% of people identified themselves as Christian. Much smaller proportions identified themselves as Muslim (4%) , Hindu (2%), Sikh (1%), Jewish or Buddhist (both less than 0.5%), and 2% of people followed another religion.

question_en: When does the Edinburgh Festival take place?
explanation_en: ﻿The Edinburgh Festival takes place in Edinburgh, Scotland, every summer . It is a series of different arts and cultural festivals, with the biggest and most well-known being the Edinburgh Festival Fringe (‘the Fringe’).

question_en: Who directed the movie ‘Brief Encounter’ in 1945?
explanation_en: Brief Encounter (1945) was directed by David Lean .

question_en: According to the 2011 Census, what percentage of the population identified themselves as Hindu?
explanation_en: According to the 2011 Census, 1.5% of the people identify themselves as Hindu.

question_en: Which landscape architect designed grounds around country houses so that the landscape appeared to be natural, with grass, trees and lakes?
explanation_en: In the 18th century, Lancelot ‘Capability’ Brown designed the grounds around country houses so that the landscape appeared to be natural, with grass, trees and lakes. He often worked with Edwin Lutyens to design colourful gardens around the houses he designed.

question_en: Which flower is associated with Scotland?
explanation_en: The thistle is the flower associated with Scotland.

question_en: What is the money from TV licences used for?

options.en: To pay for the British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC)

explanation_en: The money from TV licences is used to pay for the British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC) . This is a public service broadcaster providing television and radio programmes. The BBC is the largest broadcaster in the world. It is the only wholly state-funded media organisation that is independent of government.

question_en: Where is the centrepiece to the Remembrance Day service known as Cenotaph located?
explanation_en: The Cenotaph, the Centrepiece to the Remembrance Day service, is located in Whitehall, London .

question_en: Where was the first tennis club founded in the UK located?
explanation_en: The first tennis club was founded in Leamington Spa in 1872.

question_en: Which famous poem tells the story of the knights at the court of King Arthur?

options.en: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight

explanation_en: Poems which survive from the Middle Ages include Chaucer’s Canterbury Tales and a poem called Sir Gawain and the Green Knight , about one of the knights at the court of King Arthur.
